He then secretly descended from the mountain under the cover of night, crossing the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川|12=draft}} via the {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|12=draft}} to outflank his enemy. According to legend, Uesugi’s troops performed {{ill|Sarugaku|ja|猿楽|12=draft}} on an ancient Kofun at the summit, and he lodged at {{ill|Ruriji Temple|ja|瑠璃寺|12=draft}}, a temple along the gentle western slope toward {{ill|Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|土口将軍塚古墳|12=draft}}. The temple was later burned down by the Takeda forces after the battle. Currently, the location labeled as "Saijo-san" on maps—where an observation deck and battle explanation panels are installed—is actually a ridge summit of Saijo-zan (historically called "Akasaka" or "Akasaka-yama"). This site was where {{ill|Amakasu Kagemochi|ja|甘粕景持|12=draft}}, a rearguard commander in Uesugi’s army, set up his encampment. The site also contains a memorial hall and a monument honoring 960 war dead from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|12=draft}} onward. This was built because, during the late Edo period, {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩|12=draft}} warriors used the area for firearms training, leading to the Matsushiro lords establishing a shrine. Since the Meiji era, this site became a location for large-scale spring and autumn festivals honoring those who contributed to the establishment of the new government. As a result, "going to Saijo-san" became synonymous with visiting this location. However, the {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=draft}} worship hall was demolished in December 2023. == Naming Controversy == The modern name "Saijo-san" (妻女山, pronounced "Saijozan") became widespread from Edo-period dramas, {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kodan|ja|講談|12=draft}} narratives, which romanticized the story that Uesugi’s warriors gazed northward across Kawanakajima, longing for their distant homeland and mourning their wives and families left behind. Historical records such as the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑|12=draft}}'' refer to the encampment site as "Saijo-yama" (西條山). In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩|12=draft}} (ruled by the {{ill|Sanada clan|ja|真田氏|12=draft}}) criticized this as an error, stating in the ''Shinbu Naiden'' (真武内伝) that "Saijo-yama is not Saijo-san; they are different mountains." Nevertheless, Edo-period maps continued to use the name "Saijo-yama" or "Saijo-san." In reality, the true "Saijo-yama" lies about 10 km to the south. It was used as a signal tower where the Takeda forces sent emergency fire signals to Kai Province when the Uesugi army invaded. It remains unclear why the Matsushiro Domain equated Saijo-yama with Saijo-san. However, some local theories, even today, suggest that Uesugi's true encampment was at "Saijo-zan" (斎場山), a site that contains numerous ancient tombs and was historically used for rituals honoring the dead. Saijo-zan is part of the Togami Mountain Range (also called Kurahone Mountain Range or Omine Mountain Range). It features two ancient fortresses: the upper Kurahone Castle (鞍骨城) and the lower Amagi Castle (天城城, also known as Irayama Castle). The ridge extends east-west, with its highest point being identified as Saijo-zan. However, some maps list the area as "Akasaka" (赤坂) and label "Saijo-san" instead, effectively erasing the original name of the main peak. Some theories support retaining the name "Saijo-san." According to local legend, the Kofun at the peak of Saijo-zan is the grave of {{ill|Aizu-hime|ja|会津比売命|12=draft}}, enshrined at the nearby {{ill|Aizu-hime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社|12=draft}}. Locals also refer to the peak as "Tenjo" (天上) or "Otenjo" (御天上). Aizu-hime is believed to be the granddaughter of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神|12=draft}} and the wife of {{ill|Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|12=draft}}, the legendary first {{ill|Governor of Shinano|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=draft}}, who is associated with the {{ill|Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|12=draft}}. This theory argues that the name "Saijo-san" is justified in this context. == Alternative Historical Interpretation == While it is commonly believed that Uesugi Kenshin encamped on Saijo-san, some scholars argue that there is no historical evidence for this. They propose that it is a case of mistaken history, confused with a later military action. In March {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|12=draft}} 10 (1582), after {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長|12=draft}}'s conquest of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mori Nagayoshi|ja|森長可|12=draft}} briefly occupied northern Shinano but withdrew following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変|12=draft}}. In the ensuing {{ill|Tenshō Jingo War|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=draft}}, as {{ill|Hōjō Ujimasa|ja|北条氏直|12=draft}} advanced northward, {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|ja|上杉景勝|12=draft}} moved from {{ill|Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|ja|長沼城|12=draft}} to Kaizu Castle, establishing defensive positions at Saijo-san, Kurahone Castle, and Akazawa-yama. This forced the Hōjō forces to retreat and negotiate peace. Some historians argue that later records mistakenly conflated this event with the Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima. == Access and Wildlife == A paved road leads up to Akasaka Observation Deck, making it accessible by regular vehicles. Along the way, visitors may encounter {{ill|Japanese serow|ja|ニホンカモシカ|12=draft}}, and tree trunks bear marks from wild {{ill|boar|ja|猪|12=draft}} rubbing against them. While an unpaved road continues toward Saijo-san’s summit, it is difficult to traverse without a 4WD vehicle. During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|12=draft}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|12=draft}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|12=draft}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|12=draft}} * {{ill|Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizuhime no Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|12=draft}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) - As a result, "going to Saijo-san" became synonymous with visiting this location. However, the {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=draft}} worship hall was demolished in December 2023. == Naming Controversy == The modern name "Saijo-san" (妻女山, pronounced "Saijozan") became widespread from Edo-period dramas, {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kodan|ja|講談|12=draft}} narratives, which romanticized the story that Uesugi’s warriors gazed northward across Kawanakajima, longing for their distant homeland and mourning their wives and families left behind. Historical records such as the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑|12=draft}}'' refer to the encampment site as "Saijo-yama" (西條山). In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩|12=draft}} (ruled by the {{ill|Sanada clan|ja|真田氏|12=draft}}) criticized this as an error, stating in the ''Shinbu Naiden'' (真武内伝) that "Saijo-yama is not Saijo-san; they are different mountains." Nevertheless, Edo-period maps continued to use the name "Saijo-yama" or "Saijo-san." In reality, the true "Saijo-yama" lies about 10 km to the south. It was used as a signal tower where the Takeda forces sent emergency fire signals to Kai Province when the Uesugi army invaded. It remains unclear why the Matsushiro Domain equated Saijo-yama with Saijo-san. However, some local theories, even today, suggest that Uesugi's true encampment was at "Saijo-zan" (斎場山), a site that contains numerous ancient tombs and was historically used for rituals honoring the dead. Saijo-zan is part of the Togami Mountain Range (also called Kurahone Mountain Range or Omine Mountain Range). It features two ancient fortresses: the upper Kurahone Castle (鞍骨城) and the lower Amagi Castle (天城城, also known as Irayama Castle). The ridge extends east-west, with its highest point being identified as Saijo-zan. However, some maps list the area as "Akasaka" (赤坂) and label "Saijo-san" instead, effectively erasing the original name of the main peak. Some theories support retaining the name "Saijo-san." According to local legend, the Kofun at the peak of Saijo-zan is the grave of {{ill|Aizu-hime|ja|会津比売命|12=draft}}, enshrined at the nearby {{ill|Aizu-hime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社|12=draft}}. Locals also refer to the peak as "Tenjo" (天上) or "Otenjo" (御天上). Aizu-hime is believed to be the granddaughter of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神|12=draft}} and the wife of {{ill|Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|12=draft}}, the legendary first {{ill|Governor of Shinano|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=draft}}, who is associated with the {{ill|Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|12=draft}}. This theory argues that the name "Saijo-san" is justified in this context. == Alternative Historical Interpretation == While it is commonly believed that Uesugi Kenshin encamped on Saijo-san, some scholars argue that there is no historical evidence for this. They propose that it is a case of mistaken history, confused with a later military action. In March {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|12=draft}} 10 (1582), after {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長|12=draft}}'s conquest of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mori Nagayoshi|ja|森長可|12=draft}} briefly occupied northern Shinano but withdrew following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変|12=draft}}. In the ensuing {{ill|Tenshō Jingo War|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=draft}}, as {{ill|Hōjō Ujimasa|ja|北条氏直|12=draft}} advanced northward, {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|ja|上杉景勝|12=draft}} moved from {{ill|Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|ja|長沼城|12=draft}} to Kaizu Castle, establishing defensive positions at Saijo-san, Kurahone Castle, and Akazawa-yama. This forced the Hōjō forces to retreat and negotiate peace. Some historians argue that later records mistakenly conflated this event with the Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima. == Access and Wildlife == A paved road leads up to Akasaka Observation Deck, making it accessible by regular vehicles. Along the way, visitors may encounter {{ill|Japanese serow|ja|ニホンカモシカ|12=draft}}, and tree trunks bear marks from wild {{ill|boar|ja|猪|12=draft}} rubbing against them. While an unpaved road continues toward Saijo-san’s summit, it is difficult to traverse without a 4WD vehicle. During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|12=draft}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|12=draft}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|12=draft}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|12=draft}} * {{ill|Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizuhime no Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|12=draft}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) - {{Infobox mountain | Name = Saijo-san (妻女山) | image = [[Image:Saijyosan 1.jpg|300px|Saijo-san]] | image Caption = View of Saijo-san (Akasaka-yama) from the direction of [[Iwano Station]] | Elevation = 411 m | Coordinates = {{Coord|36|33|40.7|N|138|10|17.2|E|type:mountain_region:JP-20|display=inline,title}} | Location = {{JPN}}
{{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nagano City|ja|長野市|12=draft}} | Mountain Range = | Type = | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|36.5613|138.1714}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Saijo-san }} '''Saijo-san''' (妻女山) is a mountain located on the border between {{ill|Matsushiro, Nagano|ja|松代町|12=draft}} in {{ill|Nagano City|ja|長野市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Chikuma City|ja|千曲市|12=draft}}, Tsuchiguchi. It is best known as the location where {{ill|Uesugi Kenshin|ja|上杉謙信|12=draft}} established his encampment during the {{ill|Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima|ja|川中島の戦い|12=draft}}. However, alternative theories suggest the true encampment was on '''Saijo-zan''' (斎場山). == Overview == Saijo-san is situated in a strategic location overlooking {{ill|Kawanakajima|ja|川中島|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kaizu Castle|ja|海津城|12=draft}}, which was built by {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen|12=draft}}. It is said that Uesugi Kenshin observed the rising smoke from Kaizu Castle’s many cooking fires and discerned the movements of the Takeda forces. He then secretly descended from the mountain under the cover of night, crossing the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川|12=draft}} via the {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|12=draft}} to outflank his enemy. According to legend, Uesugi’s troops performed {{ill|Sarugaku|ja|猿楽|12=draft}} on an ancient Kofun at the summit, and he lodged at {{ill|Ruriji Temple|ja|瑠璃寺|12=draft}}, a temple along the gentle western slope toward {{ill|Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|土口将軍塚古墳|12=draft}}. The temple was later burned down by the Takeda forces after the battle. Currently, the location labeled as "Saijo-san" on maps—where an observation deck and battle explanation panels are installed—is actually a ridge summit of Saijo-zan (historically called "Akasaka" or "Akasaka-yama"). This site was where {{ill|Amakasu Kagemochi|ja|甘粕景持|12=draft}}, a rearguard commander in Uesugi’s army, set up his encampment. The site also contains a memorial hall and a monument honoring 960 war dead from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|12=draft}} onward. This was built because, during the late Edo period, {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩|12=draft}} warriors used the area for firearms training, leading to the Matsushiro lords establishing a shrine. Since the Meiji era, this site became a location for large-scale spring and autumn festivals honoring those who contributed to the establishment of the new government. As a result, "going to Saijo-san" became synonymous with visiting this location. However, the {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=draft}} worship hall was demolished in December 2023. == Naming Controversy == The modern name "Saijo-san" (妻女山, pronounced "Saijozan") became widespread from Edo-period dramas, {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kodan|ja|講談|12=draft}} narratives, which romanticized the story that Uesugi’s warriors gazed northward across Kawanakajima, longing for their distant homeland and mourning their wives and families left behind. Nevertheless, Edo-period maps continued to use the name "Saijo-yama" or "Saijo-san." In reality, the true "Saijo-yama" lies about 10 km to the south. It was used as a signal tower where the Takeda forces sent emergency fire signals to Kai Province when the Uesugi army invaded. It remains unclear why the Matsushiro Domain equated Saijo-yama with Saijo-san. However, some local theories, even today, suggest that Uesugi's true encampment was at "Saijo-zan" (斎場山), a site that contains numerous ancient tombs and was historically used for rituals honoring the dead. Saijo-zan is part of the Togami Mountain Range (also called Kurahone Mountain Range or Omine Mountain Range). It features two ancient fortresses: the upper Kurahone Castle (鞍骨城) and the lower Amagi Castle (天城城, also known as Irayama Castle). The ridge extends east-west, with its highest point being identified as Saijo-zan. However, some maps list the area as "Akasaka" (赤坂) and label "Saijo-san" instead, effectively erasing the original name of the main peak. Some theories support retaining the name "Saijo-san." According to local legend, the Kofun at the peak of Saijo-zan is the grave of {{ill|Aizu-hime|ja|会津比売命|12=draft}}, enshrined at the nearby {{ill|Aizu-hime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社|12=draft}}. Locals also refer to the peak as "Tenjo" (天上) or "Otenjo" (御天上). Aizu-hime is believed to be the granddaughter of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神|12=draft}} and the wife of {{ill|Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|12=draft}}, the legendary first {{ill|Governor of Shinano|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=draft}}, who is associated with the {{ill|Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|12=draft}}. This theory argues that the name "Saijo-san" is justified in this context. == Alternative Historical Interpretation == While it is commonly believed that Uesugi Kenshin encamped on Saijo-san, some scholars argue that there is no historical evidence for this. They propose that it is a case of mistaken history, confused with a later military action. It remains unclear why the Matsushiro Domain equated Saijo-yama with Saijo-san. However, some local theories, even today, suggest that Uesugi's true encampment was at "Saijo-zan" (斎場山), a site that contains numerous ancient tombs and was historically used for rituals honoring the dead. Saijo-zan is part of the Togami Mountain Range (also called Kurahone Mountain Range or Omine Mountain Range). It features two ancient fortresses: the upper Kurahone Castle (鞍骨城) and the lower Amagi Castle (天城城, also known as Irayama Castle). The ridge extends east-west, with its highest point being identified as Saijo-zan. However, some maps list the area as "Akasaka" (赤坂) and label "Saijo-san" instead, effectively erasing the original name of the main peak. Some theories support retaining the name "Saijo-san." According to local legend, the Kofun at the peak of Saijo-zan is the grave of {{ill|Aizu-hime|ja|会津比売命|12=draft|12=draft}}, enshrined at the nearby {{ill|Aizu-hime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社|12=draft|12=draft}}. Locals also refer to the peak as "Tenjo" (天上) or "Otenjo" (御天上). Aizu-hime is believed to be the granddaughter of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神|12=draft|12=draft}} and the wife of {{ill|Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|12=draft|12=draft}}, the legendary first {{ill|Governor of Shinano|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=draft|12=draft}}, who is associated with the {{ill|Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|12=draft|12=draft}}. This theory argues that the name "Saijo-san" is justified in this context. == Alternative Historical Interpretation == While it is commonly believed that Uesugi Kenshin encamped on Saijo-san, some scholars argue that there is no historical evidence for this. They propose that it is a case of mistaken history, confused with a later military action. In March {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|12=draft|12=draft}} 10 (1582), after {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長|12=draft|12=draft}}'s conquest of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mori Nagayoshi|ja|森長可|12=draft|12=draft}} briefly occupied northern Shinano but withdrew following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変|12=draft|12=draft}}. In the ensuing {{ill|Tenshō Jingo War|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=draft|12=draft}}, as {{ill|Hōjō Ujimasa|ja|北条氏直|12=draft|12=draft}} advanced northward, {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|ja|上杉景勝|12=draft|12=draft}} moved from {{ill|Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|ja|長沼城|12=draft|12=draft}} to Kaizu Castle, establishing defensive positions at Saijo-san, Kurahone Castle, and Akazawa-yama. This forced the Hōjō forces to retreat and negotiate peace. Some historians argue that later records mistakenly conflated this event with the Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima. == Access and Wildlife == A paved road leads up to Akasaka Observation Deck, making it accessible by regular vehicles. Along the way, visitors may encounter {{ill|Japanese serow|ja|ニホンカモシカ|12=draft|12=draft}}, and tree trunks bear marks from wild {{ill|boar|ja|猪|12=draft|12=draft}} rubbing against them. While an unpaved road continues toward Saijo-san’s summit, it is difficult to traverse without a 4WD vehicle. During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|12=draft|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizuhime no Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|12=draft|12=draft}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) - {{Infobox mountain | Name = Saijo-san (妻女山) | image = [[Image:Saijyosan 1.jpg|300px|Saijo-san]] | image Caption = View of Saijo-san (Akasaka-yama) from the direction of [[Iwano Station]] | Elevation = 411 m | Coordinates = {{Coord|36|33|40.7|N|138|10|17.2|E|type:mountain_region:JP-20|display=inline,title}} | Location = {{JPN}}
{{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nagano City|ja|長野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Mountain Range = | Type = | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|36.5613|138.1714}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Saijo-san }} '''Saijo-san''' (妻女山) is a mountain located on the border between {{ill|Matsushiro, Nagano|ja|松代町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Nagano City|ja|長野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Chikuma City|ja|千曲市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Tsuchiguchi. It is best known as the location where {{ill|Uesugi Kenshin|ja|上杉謙信|qq=draft|qq=draft}} established his encampment during the {{ill|Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima|ja|川中島の戦い|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, alternative theories suggest the true encampment was on '''Saijo-zan''' (斎場山). == Overview == Saijo-san is situated in a strategic location overlooking {{ill|Kawanakajima|ja|川中島|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kaizu Castle|ja|海津城|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which was built by {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is said that Uesugi Kenshin observed the rising smoke from Kaizu Castle’s many cooking fires and discerned the movements of the Takeda forces. He then secretly descended from the mountain under the cover of night, crossing the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川|qq=draft|qq=draft}} via the {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to outflank his enemy. According to legend, Uesugi’s troops performed {{ill|Sarugaku|ja|猿楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on an ancient Kofun at the summit, and he lodged at {{ill|Ruriji Temple|ja|瑠璃寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a temple along the gentle western slope toward {{ill|Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|土口将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The temple was later burned down by the Takeda forces after the battle. Currently, the location labeled as "Saijo-san" on maps—where an observation deck and battle explanation panels are installed—is actually a ridge summit of Saijo-zan (historically called "Akasaka" or "Akasaka-yama"). This site was where {{ill|Amakasu Kagemochi|ja|甘粕景持|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a rearguard commander in Uesugi’s army, set up his encampment. The site also contains a memorial hall and a monument honoring 960 war dead from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}} onward. This was built because, during the late Edo period, {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} warriors used the area for firearms training, leading to the Matsushiro lords establishing a shrine. Since the Meiji era, this site became a location for large-scale spring and autumn festivals honoring those who contributed to the establishment of the new government. As a result, "going to Saijo-san" became synonymous with visiting this location. However, the {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} worship hall was demolished in December 2023. == Naming Controversy == The modern name "Saijo-san" (妻女山, pronounced "Saijozan") became widespread from Edo-period dramas, {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kodan|ja|講談|qq=draft|qq=draft}} narratives, which romanticized the story that Uesugi’s warriors gazed northward across Kawanakajima, longing for their distant homeland and mourning their wives and families left behind. Historical records such as the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' refer to the encampment site as "Saijo-yama" (西條山). In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (ruled by the {{ill|Sanada clan|ja|真田氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) criticized this as an error, stating in the ''Shinbu Naiden'' (真武内伝) that "Saijo-yama is not Saijo-san; they are different mountains." Nevertheless, Edo-period maps continued to use the name "Saijo-yama" or "Saijo-san." In reality, the true "Saijo-yama" lies about 10 km to the south. It was used as a signal tower where the Takeda forces sent emergency fire signals to Kai Province when the Uesugi army invaded. It remains unclear why the Matsushiro Domain equated Saijo-yama with Saijo-san. However, some local theories, even today, suggest that Uesugi's true encampment was at "Saijo-zan" (斎場山), a site that contains numerous ancient tombs and was historically used for rituals honoring the dead. Saijo-zan is part of the Togami Mountain Range (also called Kurahone Mountain Range or Omine Mountain Range). It features two ancient fortresses: the upper Kurahone Castle (鞍骨城) and the lower Amagi Castle (天城城, also known as Irayama Castle). The ridge extends east-west, with its highest point being identified as Saijo-zan. However, some maps list the area as "Akasaka" (赤坂) and label "Saijo-san" instead, effectively erasing the original name of the main peak. Some theories support retaining the name "Saijo-san." According to local legend, the Kofun at the peak of Saijo-zan is the grave of {{ill|Aizu-hime|ja|会津比売命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, enshrined at the nearby {{ill|Aizu-hime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Locals also refer to the peak as "Tenjo" (天上) or "Otenjo" (御天上). Aizu-hime is believed to be the granddaughter of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the wife of {{ill|Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the legendary first {{ill|Governor of Shinano|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who is associated with the {{ill|Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This theory argues that the name "Saijo-san" is justified in this context. == Alternative Historical Interpretation == While it is commonly believed that Uesugi Kenshin encamped on Saijo-san, some scholars argue that there is no historical evidence for this. They propose that it is a case of mistaken history, confused with a later military action. In March {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 10 (1582), after {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s conquest of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Mori Nagayoshi|ja|森長可|qq=draft|qq=draft}} briefly occupied northern Shinano but withdrew following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In the ensuing {{ill|Tenshō Jingo War|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as {{ill|Hōjō Ujimasa|ja|北条氏直|qq=draft|qq=draft}} advanced northward, {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|ja|上杉景勝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} moved from {{ill|Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|ja|長沼城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to Kaizu Castle, establishing defensive positions at Saijo-san, Kurahone Castle, and Akazawa-yama. This forced the Hōjō forces to retreat and negotiate peace. Some historians argue that later records mistakenly conflated this event with the Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima. == Access and Wildlife == A paved road leads up to Akasaka Observation Deck, making it accessible by regular vehicles. Along the way, visitors may encounter {{ill|Japanese serow|ja|ニホンカモシカ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and tree trunks bear marks from wild {{ill|boar|ja|猪|qq=draft|qq=draft}} rubbing against them. While an unpaved road continues toward Saijo-san’s summit, it is difficult to traverse without a 4WD vehicle. During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizuhime no Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) - revision.text:
{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 妻女山 |画像 = [[Image:Saijyosan 1.jpg|300px|妻女山]] |画像キャプション = [[岩野駅]]方面より望む妻女山（赤坂山） |標高 = 411 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|33|40.7|N|138|10|17.2|E|type:mountain_region:JP-20|display=inline,title}} |所在地 = {{JPN}}
[[長野県]][[長野市]] |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|36.5613|138.1714}}|caption=|width=300}}妻女山の位置 }} '''妻女山'''（さいじょさん）は、[[長野県]][[長野市]][[松代町 (長野県)|松代町]]と[[千曲市]]土口が境を接する山。第4次[[川中島の戦い]]において[[上杉謙信]]の軍が陣を張った地として知られるが、異論として「'''斎場山'''」（さいじょうざん）についても併記する。 == 概要 == [[武田信玄]]が築いた[[松代城|海津城]]や[[川中島]]平を見下ろすことができる位置にあり、上杉謙信はこの山上から海津城に立ちのぼる夥しい炊煙を見て武田方の攻撃を察知したとされる。そして秘かに山を下り、夜陰に紛れて[[雨宮の渡し]]から[[千曲川]]を[[渡河]]して敵の裏をかいたと伝えられる。また頂上にある[[古墳]]上で[[猿楽]]を舞ったとの伝承や西の[[土口将軍塚古墳]]方面に下る緩斜面上にあった瑠璃寺を宿所としていたため戦後武田軍によって寺は焼き払われたとの言い伝えもある。 なお現在「妻女山」とされて展望台や甲越合戦の解説板が設置されている所は、斎場山の尾根上の頂（元は「赤坂」又は「赤坂山」と呼ばれた）であり、謙信配下の殿（しんがり）部隊[[甘粕景持|甘粕近江守景持]]が陣を敷いた場所とされる。この場所には忠霊殿、忠魂碑もあるが何れも[[戊辰戦争]]以来の戦死者960人余を祀るもので、ここで幕末期に[[松代藩]]士らが銃撃訓練をした縁で松代藩主によって建立された。そこでは明治期以来、[[明治維新|新政府]]樹立に寄与した功績を称えて盛大に春秋の祭礼が行われた。従って、以来この場所に集うことを「妻女山に行く」こととされるようになったようである。しかし、その招魂社拝殿は2023年12月に取り壊された。 == 名称について == 現在一般化している「妻女山」（さいじょざん）の表記は、上杉軍将兵が北方に開けた川中島の向こうに遠く離れた郷里への望郷の念を抱いて、残して来た「妻女を偲んで涙した」と[[江戸時代]]中期になってから[[芝居]]や[[浄瑠璃]]、[[講談]]等で語られて広まったことによるとされている。 元々上杉軍が陣を設けた場所について、『[[甲陽軍鑑]]』の記載は「西條山」（さいじょうざん）である。この記載は江戸時代中期に[[松代藩]]（[[真田氏|真田家]]）から「妻女山を西條山と書すは誤也、山も異也」（真田氏史書『真武内伝』）と指摘を受けているが、江戸期の[[地図]]は概ね「西條山」あるいは「西条山」と記載されている。そのため、[[明治]]になってから作られた唱歌「川中島」の歌いだしも「西条山は霧ふかし」である。 しかし本来「西條山」あるいは「西条山」の名がつく山は南に10kmほど離れた全く異なる山であり、川中島の戦い当時は海津城将が上杉軍来襲を甲斐に[[狼煙]]で急報した烽火台があった場所とされている。松代藩が「西條山＝妻女山」とした理由については不明であるが、西條山と同じ読みである斎場山が上杉軍が陣を設けた場所とする説が、現在でも地元を中心に残されている（長野市の「信州・風林火山」特設サイトでも紹介されている[http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php]{{リンク切れ|date=2023年2月}}）。 「斎場山」と周辺には多くの[[古墳]]が存在（[[埴科古墳群]]参照）し、古代から死者を弔い祭る山とされ「斎場」もしくは「祭場」と表記するのが正しいとされる。斎場山は戸神山脈（もしくは鞍骨山脈、大峰山脈とも）にある上部の[[鞍骨城]]（くらほねじょう）と、その下部の[[天城城]]（てしろじょうー入山城とも）の二つの山城を北に下った尾根先からＴ字形に東西へ伸びる山脈を形成している。その東端は赤坂で西端が笹崎、中央部の主峰が謙信布陣と伝えられる斎場山である。しかし斎場山や薬師山と表記されている地図もあれば、「赤坂」を「妻女山」と表記して、この最高地の「斎場山」を無名にしている地図もある。無名にしてある地図の方がむしろ多い。 この主峰を「妻女山」のままが良しとする説もある。それによれば「斎場山」山頂の古墳（直径４０メートルの[[円墳]]）は、麓にある「[[会津比売神社]]」の祭神「[[会津比売神|会津比売命]]」の墓と伝説される。そして地元では「天上」もしくは「御天上」とも言うのだといい「会津比売命」は[[建御名方神]]の孫娘に当たるとする。初代の[[科野国造]]である「[[武五百建命]]（伝説では[[森将軍塚古墳]]の被葬者）」の妻であるので「妻女」の山が正しいとするものである。 == その他 == 上杉謙信の妻女山布陣は定説となっているが「史実にはない」と否定するむきもある。天正10年（[[1582年]]）3月、甲斐武田家を滅ぼし（[[武田征伐]]）、北信濃を領有した織田軍の[[森長可]]が[[本能寺の変]]で[[織田信長]]の後ろ盾を失って僅か3か月で撤退後、徳川、北条らとの三つ巴（[[天正壬午の乱]]）の草刈場と化した[[川中島]]に、[[北条氏直]]軍の北上に備えた[[上杉景勝]]は[[長沼城 (信濃国)|長沼城]]を経由して海津城に入って妻女山・鞍骨城一帯、赤澤山（赤澤城ー[[塩崎新城]]）に布陣。これによって徳川・上杉両軍の挟撃を恐れた北条軍が上杉氏との講和をして撤退した史実との「混同」だとするのがそれである。 赤坂展望台までは舗装路があり普通乗用車でも行くことができ、途中では運がよいとニホンカモシカに遇える。付近には樹木の幹に[[猪]]が体をこすり付けた跡も多く見られる。赤坂展望台から妻女山頂上へは未舗装路があり四輪駆動車と見られる轍が見られるが普通乗用車での通行は難しい。夏季は蜂が発生する上、樹木が繁って眺望は期待できない。 == 関連項目 == * [[雨宮の渡し]] * [[屋代城]] * [[塩崎城]] * [[塩崎新城]] * [[稲荷山宿|稲荷山城]] * [[荒砥城]] * [[皆神山]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 【妻女山】長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト]…財団法人ながの観光コンベンションビューロー開設 {{DEFAULTSORT:さいしよさん}} {{mountain-stub|pref=長野県}} [[Category:山岳名目録]] [[Category:長野県の山]] [[Category:長野市の地理]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[妻女山]] to [[Tsumaonna Mountain]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 妻女山 |画像 = [[Image:Saijyosan 1.jpg|300px|妻女山]] |画像キャプション = [[岩野駅]]方面より望む妻女山（赤坂山） |標高 = 411 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|33|40.7|N|138|10|17.2|E|type:mountain_region:JP-20|display=inline,title}} |所在地 = {{JPN}}
[[長野県]][[長野市]] |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|36.5613|138.1714}}|caption=|width=300}}妻女山の位置 }} '''妻女山'''（さいじょさん）は、[[長野県]][[長野市]][[松代町 (長野県)|松代町]]と[[千曲市]]土口が境を接する山。第4次[[川中島の戦い]]において[[上杉謙信]]の軍が陣を張った地として知られるが、異論として「'''斎場山'''」（さいじょうざん）についても併記する。 == 概要 == [[武田信玄]]が築いた[[松代城|海津城]]や[[川中島]]平を見下ろすことができる位置にあり、上杉謙信はこの山上から海津城に立ちのぼる夥しい炊煙を見て武田方の攻撃を察知したとされる。そして秘かに山を下り、夜陰に紛れて[[雨宮の渡し]]から[[千曲川]]を[[渡河]]して敵の裏をかいたと伝えられる。また頂上にある[[古墳]]上で[[猿楽]]を舞ったとの伝承や西の[[土口将軍塚古墳]]方面に下る緩斜面上にあった瑠璃寺を宿所としていたため戦後武田軍によって寺は焼き払われたとの言い伝えもある。 なお現在「妻女山」とされて展望台や甲越合戦の解説板が設置されている所は、斎場山の尾根上の頂（元は「赤坂」又は「赤坂山」と呼ばれた）であり、謙信配下の殿（しんがり）部隊[[甘粕景持|甘粕近江守景持]]が陣を敷いた場所とされる。この場所には忠霊殿、忠魂碑もあるが何れも[[戊辰戦争]]以来の戦死者960人余を祀るもので、ここで幕末期に[[松代藩]]士らが銃撃訓練をした縁で松代藩主によって建立された。そこでは明治期以来、[[明治維新|新政府]]樹立に寄与した功績を称えて盛大に春秋の祭礼が行われた。従って、以来この場所に集うことを「妻女山に行く」こととされるようになったようである。しかし、その招魂社拝殿は2023年12月に取り壊された。 == 名称について == 現在一般化している「妻女山」（さいじょざん）の表記は、上杉軍将兵が北方に開けた川中島の向こうに遠く離れた郷里への望郷の念を抱いて、残して来た「妻女を偲んで涙した」と[[江戸時代]]中期になってから[[芝居]]や[[浄瑠璃]]、[[講談]]等で語られて広まったことによるとされている。 元々上杉軍が陣を設けた場所について、『[[甲陽軍鑑]]』の記載は「西條山」（さいじょうざん）である。この記載は江戸時代中期に[[松代藩]]（[[真田氏|真田家]]）から「妻女山を西條山と書すは誤也、山も異也」（真田氏史書『真武内伝』）と指摘を受けているが、江戸期の[[地図]]は概ね「西條山」あるいは「西条山」と記載されている。そのため、[[明治]]になってから作られた唱歌「川中島」の歌いだしも「西条山は霧ふかし」である。 しかし本来「西條山」あるいは「西条山」の名がつく山は南に10kmほど離れた全く異なる山であり、川中島の戦い当時は海津城将が上杉軍来襲を甲斐に[[狼煙]]で急報した烽火台があった場所とされている。松代藩が「西條山＝妻女山」とした理由については不明であるが、西條山と同じ読みである斎場山が上杉軍が陣を設けた場所とする説が、現在でも地元を中心に残されている（長野市の「信州・風林火山」特設サイトでも紹介されている[http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php]{{リンク切れ|date=2023年2月}}）。 「斎場山」と周辺には多くの[[古墳]]が存在（[[埴科古墳群]]参照）し、古代から死者を弔い祭る山とされ「斎場」もしくは「祭場」と表記するのが正しいとされる。斎場山は戸神山脈（もしくは鞍骨山脈、大峰山脈とも）にある上部の[[鞍骨城]]（くらほねじょう）と、その下部の[[天城城]]（てしろじょうー入山城とも）の二つの山城を北に下った尾根先からＴ字形に東西へ伸びる山脈を形成している。その東端は赤坂で西端が笹崎、中央部の主峰が謙信布陣と伝えられる斎場山である。しかし斎場山や薬師山と表記されている地図もあれば、「赤坂」を「妻女山」と表記して、この最高地の「斎場山」を無名にしている地図もある。無名にしてある地図の方がむしろ多い。 この主峰を「妻女山」のままが良しとする説もある。それによれば「斎場山」山頂の古墳（直径４０メートルの[[円墳]]）は、麓にある「[[会津比売神社]]」の祭神「[[会津比売神|会津比売命]]」の墓と伝説される。そして地元では「天上」もしくは「御天上」とも言うのだといい「会津比売命」は[[建御名方神]]の孫娘に当たるとする。初代の[[科野国造]]である「[[武五百建命]]（伝説では[[森将軍塚古墳]]の被葬者）」の妻であるので「妻女」の山が正しいとするものである。 == その他 == 上杉謙信の妻女山布陣は定説となっているが「史実にはない」と否定するむきもある。天正10年（[[1582年]]）3月、甲斐武田家を滅ぼし（[[武田征伐]]）、北信濃を領有した織田軍の[[森長可]]が[[本能寺の変]]で[[織田信長]]の後ろ盾を失って僅か3か月で撤退後、徳川、北条らとの三つ巴（[[天正壬午の乱]]）の草刈場と化した[[川中島]]に、[[北条氏直]]軍の北上に備えた[[上杉景勝]]は[[長沼城 (信濃国)|長沼城]]を経由して海津城に入って妻女山・鞍骨城一帯、赤澤山（赤澤城ー[[塩崎新城]]）に布陣。これによって徳川・上杉両軍の挟撃を恐れた北条軍が上杉氏との講和をして撤退した史実との「混同」だとするのがそれである。 赤坂展望台までは舗装路があり普通乗用車でも行くことができ、途中では運がよいとニホンカモシカに遇える。付近には樹木の幹に[[猪]]が体をこすり付けた跡も多く見られる。赤坂展望台から妻女山頂上へは未舗装路があり四輪駆動車と見られる轍が見られるが普通乗用車での通行は難しい。夏季は蜂が発生する上、樹木が繁って眺望は期待できない。 == 関連項目 == * [[雨宮の渡し]] * [[屋代城]] * [[塩崎城]] * [[塩崎新城]] * [[稲荷山宿|稲荷山城]] * [[荒砥城]] * [[皆神山]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 【妻女山】長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト]…財団法人ながの観光コンベンションビューロー開設 {{DEFAULTSORT:さいしよさん}} {{mountain-stub|pref=長野県}} [[Category:山岳名目録]] [[Category:長野県の山]] [[Category:長野市の地理]]

{{Infobox mountain | Name = Saijo-san (妻女山) | image = [[Image:Saijyosan 1.jpg|300px|Saijo-san]] | image Caption = View of Saijo-san (Akasaka-yama) from the direction of [[Iwano Station]] | Elevation = 411 m | Coordinates = {{Coord|36|33|40.7|N|138|10|17.2|E|type:mountain_region:JP-20|display=inline,title}} | Location = {{JPN}}
{{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nagano City|ja|長野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Mountain Range = | Type = | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|36.5613|138.1714}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Saijo-san }} '''Saijo-san''' (妻女山) is a mountain located on the border between {{ill|Matsushiro, Nagano|ja|松代町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Nagano City|ja|長野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Chikuma City|ja|千曲市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Tsuchiguchi. It is best known as the location where {{ill|Uesugi Kenshin|ja|上杉謙信|qq=draft|qq=draft}} established his encampment during the {{ill|Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima|ja|川中島の戦い|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, alternative theories suggest the true encampment was on '''Saijo-zan''' (斎場山). == Overview == Saijo-san is situated in a strategic location overlooking {{ill|Kawanakajima|ja|川中島|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kaizu Castle|ja|海津城|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which was built by {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is said that Uesugi Kenshin observed the rising smoke from Kaizu Castle’s many cooking fires and discerned the movements of the Takeda forces. He then secretly descended from the mountain under the cover of night, crossing the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川|qq=draft|qq=draft}} via the {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to outflank his enemy. According to legend, Uesugi’s troops performed {{ill|Sarugaku|ja|猿楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on an ancient Kofun at the summit, and he lodged at {{ill|Ruriji Temple|ja|瑠璃寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a temple along the gentle western slope toward {{ill|Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|土口将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The temple was later burned down by the Takeda forces after the battle. Currently, the location labeled as "Saijo-san" on maps—where an observation deck and battle explanation panels are installed—is actually a ridge summit of Saijo-zan (historically called "Akasaka" or "Akasaka-yama"). This site was where {{ill|Amakasu Kagemochi|ja|甘粕景持|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a rearguard commander in Uesugi’s army, set up his encampment. The site also contains a memorial hall and a monument honoring 960 war dead from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}} onward. This was built because, during the late Edo period, {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} warriors used the area for firearms training, leading to the Matsushiro lords establishing a shrine. Since the Meiji era, this site became a location for large-scale spring and autumn festivals honoring those who contributed to the establishment of the new government. As a result, "going to Saijo-san" became synonymous with visiting this location. However, the {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} worship hall was demolished in December 2023. == Naming Controversy == The modern name "Saijo-san" (妻女山, pronounced "Saijozan") became widespread from Edo-period dramas, {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kodan|ja|講談|qq=draft|qq=draft}} narratives, which romanticized the story that Uesugi’s warriors gazed northward across Kawanakajima, longing for their distant homeland and mourning their wives and families left behind. Historical records such as the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' refer to the encampment site as "Saijo-yama" (西條山). In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (ruled by the {{ill|Sanada clan|ja|真田氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) criticized this as an error, stating in the ''Shinbu Naiden'' (真武内伝) that "Saijo-yama is not Saijo-san; they are different mountains." Nevertheless, Edo-period maps continued to use the name "Saijo-yama" or "Saijo-san." In reality, the true "Saijo-yama" lies about 10 km to the south. It was used as a signal tower where the Takeda forces sent emergency fire signals to Kai Province when the Uesugi army invaded. It remains unclear why the Matsushiro Domain equated Saijo-yama with Saijo-san. However, some local theories, even today, suggest that Uesugi's true encampment was at "Saijo-zan" (斎場山), a site that contains numerous ancient tombs and was historically used for rituals honoring the dead. Saijo-zan is part of the Togami Mountain Range (also called Kurahone Mountain Range or Omine Mountain Range). It features two ancient fortresses: the upper Kurahone Castle (鞍骨城) and the lower Amagi Castle (天城城, also known as Irayama Castle). The ridge extends east-west, with its highest point being identified as Saijo-zan. However, some maps list the area as "Akasaka" (赤坂) and label "Saijo-san" instead, effectively erasing the original name of the main peak. Some theories support retaining the name "Saijo-san." According to local legend, the Kofun at the peak of Saijo-zan is the grave of {{ill|Aizu-hime|ja|会津比売命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, enshrined at the nearby {{ill|Aizu-hime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Locals also refer to the peak as "Tenjo" (天上) or "Otenjo" (御天上). Aizu-hime is believed to be the granddaughter of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the wife of {{ill|Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the legendary first {{ill|Governor of Shinano|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who is associated with the {{ill|Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This theory argues that the name "Saijo-san" is justified in this context. == Alternative Historical Interpretation == While it is commonly believed that Uesugi Kenshin encamped on Saijo-san, some scholars argue that there is no historical evidence for this. They propose that it is a case of mistaken history, confused with a later military action. In March {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 10 (1582), after {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s conquest of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Mori Nagayoshi|ja|森長可|qq=draft|qq=draft}} briefly occupied northern Shinano but withdrew following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In the ensuing {{ill|Tenshō Jingo War|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as {{ill|Hōjō Ujimasa|ja|北条氏直|qq=draft|qq=draft}} advanced northward, {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|ja|上杉景勝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} moved from {{ill|Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|ja|長沼城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to Kaizu Castle, establishing defensive positions at Saijo-san, Kurahone Castle, and Akazawa-yama. This forced the Hōjō forces to retreat and negotiate peace. Some historians argue that later records mistakenly conflated this event with the Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima. == Access and Wildlife == A paved road leads up to Akasaka Observation Deck, making it accessible by regular vehicles. Along the way, visitors may encounter {{ill|Japanese serow|ja|ニホンカモシカ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and tree trunks bear marks from wild {{ill|boar|ja|猪|qq=draft|qq=draft}} rubbing against them. While an unpaved road continues toward Saijo-san’s summit, it is difficult to traverse without a 4WD vehicle. During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizuhime no Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) - {{Infobox mountain | Name = Saijo-san (妻女山) | image = [[Image:Saijyosan 1.jpg|300px|Saijo-san]] | image Caption = View of Saijo-san (Akasaka-yama) from the direction of [[Iwano Station]] | Elevation = 411 m | Coordinates = {{Coord|36|33|40.7|N|138|10|17.2|E|type:mountain_region:JP-20|display=inline,title}} | Location = {{JPN}}
{{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nagano City|ja|長野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Mountain Range = | Type = | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|36.5613|138.1714}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Saijo-san }} '''Saijo-san''' (妻女山) is a mountain located on the border between {{ill|Matsushiro, Nagano|ja|松代町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Nagano City|ja|長野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Chikuma City|ja|千曲市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Tsuchiguchi. It is best known as the location where {{ill|Uesugi Kenshin|ja|上杉謙信|qq=draft|qq=draft}} established his encampment during the {{ill|Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima|ja|川中島の戦い|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, alternative theories suggest the true encampment was on '''Saijo-zan''' (斎場山). == Overview == Saijo-san is situated in a strategic location overlooking {{ill|Kawanakajima|ja|川中島|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kaizu Castle|ja|海津城|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which was built by {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is said that Uesugi Kenshin observed the rising smoke from Kaizu Castle’s many cooking fires and discerned the movements of the Takeda forces. He then secretly descended from the mountain under the cover of night, crossing the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川|qq=draft|qq=draft}} via the {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to outflank his enemy. According to legend, Uesugi’s troops performed {{ill|Sarugaku|ja|猿楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on an ancient Kofun at the summit, and he lodged at {{ill|Ruriji Temple|ja|瑠璃寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a temple along the gentle western slope toward {{ill|Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|土口将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The temple was later burned down by the Takeda forces after the battle. Currently, the location labeled as "Saijo-san" on maps—where an observation deck and battle explanation panels are installed—is actually a ridge summit of Saijo-zan (historically called "Akasaka" or "Akasaka-yama"). This site was where {{ill|Amakasu Kagemochi|ja|甘粕景持|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a rearguard commander in Uesugi’s army, set up his encampment. The site also contains a memorial hall and a monument honoring 960 war dead from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}} onward. This was built because, during the late Edo period, {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} warriors used the area for firearms training, leading to the Matsushiro lords establishing a shrine. Since the Meiji era, this site became a location for large-scale spring and autumn festivals honoring those who contributed to the establishment of the new government. As a result, "going to Saijo-san" became synonymous with visiting this location. However, the {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} worship hall was demolished in December 2023. == Naming Controversy == The modern name "Saijo-san" (妻女山, pronounced "Saijozan") became widespread from Edo-period dramas, {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kodan|ja|講談|qq=draft|qq=draft}} narratives, which romanticized the story that Uesugi’s warriors gazed northward across Kawanakajima, longing for their distant homeland and mourning their wives and families left behind. Historical records such as the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' refer to the encampment site as "Saijo-yama" (西條山). In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (ruled by the {{ill|Sanada clan|ja|真田氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) criticized this as an error, stating in the ''Shinbu Naiden'' (真武内伝) that "Saijo-yama is not Saijo-san; they are different mountains." Nevertheless, Edo-period maps continued to use the name "Saijo-yama" or "Saijo-san." In reality, the true "Saijo-yama" lies about 10 km to the south. It was used as a signal tower where the Takeda forces sent emergency fire signals to Kai Province when the Uesugi army invaded. It remains unclear why the Matsushiro Domain equated Saijo-yama with Saijo-san. However, some local theories, even today, suggest that Uesugi's true encampment was at "Saijo-zan" (斎場山), a site that contains numerous ancient tombs and was historically used for rituals honoring the dead. Saijo-zan is part of the Togami Mountain Range (also called Kurahone Mountain Range or Omine Mountain Range). It features two ancient fortresses: the upper Kurahone Castle (鞍骨城) and the lower Amagi Castle (天城城, also known as Irayama Castle). The ridge extends east-west, with its highest point being identified as Saijo-zan. However, some maps list the area as "Akasaka" (赤坂) and label "Saijo-san" instead, effectively erasing the original name of the main peak. Some theories support retaining the name "Saijo-san." According to local legend, the Kofun at the peak of Saijo-zan is the grave of {{ill|Aizu-hime|ja|会津比売命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, enshrined at the nearby {{ill|Aizu-hime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Locals also refer to the peak as "Tenjo" (天上) or "Otenjo" (御天上). Aizu-hime is believed to be the granddaughter of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the wife of {{ill|Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the legendary first {{ill|Governor of Shinano|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who is associated with the {{ill|Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This theory argues that the name "Saijo-san" is justified in this context. == Alternative Historical Interpretation == While it is commonly believed that Uesugi Kenshin encamped on Saijo-san, some scholars argue that there is no historical evidence for this. They propose that it is a case of mistaken history, confused with a later military action. In March {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 10 (1582), after {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s conquest of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Mori Nagayoshi|ja|森長可|qq=draft|qq=draft}} briefly occupied northern Shinano but withdrew following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In the ensuing {{ill|Tenshō Jingo War|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as {{ill|Hōjō Ujimasa|ja|北条氏直|qq=draft|qq=draft}} advanced northward, {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|ja|上杉景勝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} moved from {{ill|Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|ja|長沼城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to Kaizu Castle, establishing defensive positions at Saijo-san, Kurahone Castle, and Akazawa-yama. This forced the Hōjō forces to retreat and negotiate peace. Some historians argue that later records mistakenly conflated this event with the Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima. == Access and Wildlife == A paved road leads up to Akasaka Observation Deck, making it accessible by regular vehicles. Along the way, visitors may encounter {{ill|Japanese serow|ja|ニホンカモシカ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and tree trunks bear marks from wild {{ill|boar|ja|猪|qq=draft|qq=draft}} rubbing against them. While an unpaved road continues toward Saijo-san’s summit, it is difficult to traverse without a 4WD vehicle. During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizuhime no Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) - During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizuhime no Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) - {{Infobox mountain | Name = Saijo-san (妻女山) | image = [[Image:Saijyosan 1.jpg|300px|Saijo-san]] | image Caption = View of Saijo-san (Akasaka-yama) from the direction of [[Iwano Station]] | Elevation = 411 m | Coordinates = {{Coord|36|33|40.7|N|138|10|17.2|E|type:mountain_region:JP-20|display=inline,title}} | Location = {{JPN}}
{{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nagano City|ja|長野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Mountain Range = | Type = | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|36.5613|138.1714}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Saijo-san }} '''Saijo-san''' (妻女山) is a mountain located on the border between {{ill|Matsushiro, Nagano|ja|松代町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Nagano City|ja|長野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Chikuma City|ja|千曲市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Tsuchiguchi. It is best known as the location where {{ill|Uesugi Kenshin|ja|上杉謙信|qq=draft|qq=draft}} established his encampment during the {{ill|Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima|ja|川中島の戦い|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, alternative theories suggest the true encampment was on '''Saijo-zan''' (斎場山). == Overview == Saijo-san is situated in a strategic location overlooking {{ill|Kawanakajima|ja|川中島|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kaizu Castle|ja|海津城|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which was built by {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is said that Uesugi Kenshin observed the rising smoke from Kaizu Castle’s many cooking fires and discerned the movements of the Takeda forces. He then secretly descended from the mountain under the cover of night, crossing the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川|qq=draft|qq=draft}} via the {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to outflank his enemy. According to legend, Uesugi’s troops performed {{ill|Sarugaku|ja|猿楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on an ancient Kofun at the summit, and he lodged at {{ill|Ruriji Temple|ja|瑠璃寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a temple along the gentle western slope toward {{ill|Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|土口将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The temple was later burned down by the Takeda forces after the battle. Currently, the location labeled as "Saijo-san" on maps—where an observation deck and battle explanation panels are installed—is actually a ridge summit of Saijo-zan (historically called "Akasaka" or "Akasaka-yama"). This site was where {{ill|Amakasu Kagemochi|ja|甘粕景持|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a rearguard commander in Uesugi’s army, set up his encampment. The site also contains a memorial hall and a monument honoring 960 war dead from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}} onward. This was built because, during the late Edo period, {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} warriors used the area for firearms training, leading to the Matsushiro lords establishing a shrine. Since the Meiji era, this site became a location for large-scale spring and autumn festivals honoring those who contributed to the establishment of the new government. As a result, "going to Saijo-san" became synonymous with visiting this location. However, the {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} worship hall was demolished in December 2023. == Naming Controversy == The modern name "Saijo-san" (妻女山, pronounced "Saijozan") became widespread from Edo-period dramas, {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kodan|ja|講談|qq=draft|qq=draft}} narratives, which romanticized the story that Uesugi’s warriors gazed northward across Kawanakajima, longing for their distant homeland and mourning their wives and families left behind. Historical records such as the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' refer to the encampment site as "Saijo-yama" (西條山). In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (ruled by the {{ill|Sanada clan|ja|真田氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) criticized this as an error, stating in the ''Shinbu Naiden'' (真武内伝) that "Saijo-yama is not Saijo-san; they are different mountains." Nevertheless, Edo-period maps continued to use the name "Saijo-yama" or "Saijo-san." In reality, the true "Saijo-yama" lies about 10 km to the south. It was used as a signal tower where the Takeda forces sent emergency fire signals to Kai Province when the Uesugi army invaded. It remains unclear why the Matsushiro Domain equated Saijo-yama with Saijo-san. However, some local theories, even today, suggest that Uesugi's true encampment was at "Saijo-zan" (斎場山), a site that contains numerous ancient tombs and was historically used for rituals honoring the dead. Saijo-zan is part of the Togami Mountain Range (also called Kurahone Mountain Range or Omine Mountain Range). It features two ancient fortresses: the upper Kurahone Castle (鞍骨城) and the lower Amagi Castle (天城城, also known as Irayama Castle). The ridge extends east-west, with its highest point being identified as Saijo-zan. However, some maps list the area as "Akasaka" (赤坂) and label "Saijo-san" instead, effectively erasing the original name of the main peak. Some theories support retaining the name "Saijo-san." According to local legend, the Kofun at the peak of Saijo-zan is the grave of {{ill|Aizu-hime|ja|会津比売命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, enshrined at the nearby {{ill|Aizu-hime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Locals also refer to the peak as "Tenjo" (天上) or "Otenjo" (御天上). Aizu-hime is believed to be the granddaughter of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the wife of {{ill|Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the legendary first {{ill|Governor of Shinano|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who is associated with the {{ill|Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This theory argues that the name "Saijo-san" is justified in this context. == Alternative Historical Interpretation == While it is commonly believed that Uesugi Kenshin encamped on Saijo-san, some scholars argue that there is no historical evidence for this. They propose that it is a case of mistaken history, confused with a later military action. In March {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 10 (1582), after {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s conquest of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Mori Nagayoshi|ja|森長可|qq=draft|qq=draft}} briefly occupied northern Shinano but withdrew following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In the ensuing {{ill|Tenshō Jingo War|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as {{ill|Hōjō Ujimasa|ja|北条氏直|qq=draft|qq=draft}} advanced northward, {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|ja|上杉景勝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} moved from {{ill|Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|ja|長沼城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to Kaizu Castle, establishing defensive positions at Saijo-san, Kurahone Castle, and Akazawa-yama. This forced the Hōjō forces to retreat and negotiate peace. Some historians argue that later records mistakenly conflated this event with the Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima. == Access and Wildlife == A paved road leads up to Akasaka Observation Deck, making it accessible by regular vehicles. Along the way, visitors may encounter {{ill|Japanese serow|ja|ニホンカモシカ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and tree trunks bear marks from wild {{ill|boar|ja|猪|qq=draft|qq=draft}} rubbing against them. While an unpaved road continues toward Saijo-san’s summit, it is difficult to traverse without a 4WD vehicle. During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizuhime no Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) - During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizuhime no Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) - {{Infobox mountain | Name = Saijo-san (妻女山) | image = [[Image:Saijyosan 1.jpg|300px|Saijo-san]] | image Caption = View of Saijo-san (Akasaka-yama) from the direction of [[Iwano Station]] | Elevation = 411 m | Coordinates = {{Coord|36|33|40.7|N|138|10|17.2|E|type:mountain_region:JP-20|display=inline,title}} | Location = {{JPN}}
{{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nagano City|ja|長野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Mountain Range = | Type = | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|36.5613|138.1714}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Saijo-san }} '''Saijo-san''' (妻女山) is a mountain located on the border between {{ill|Matsushiro, Nagano|ja|松代町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Nagano City|ja|長野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Chikuma City|ja|千曲市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Tsuchiguchi. It is best known as the location where {{ill|Uesugi Kenshin|ja|上杉謙信|qq=draft|qq=draft}} established his encampment during the {{ill|Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima|ja|川中島の戦い|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, alternative theories suggest the true encampment was on '''Saijo-zan''' (斎場山). == Overview == Saijo-san is situated in a strategic location overlooking {{ill|Kawanakajima|ja|川中島|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kaizu Castle|ja|海津城|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which was built by {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is said that Uesugi Kenshin observed the rising smoke from Kaizu Castle’s many cooking fires and discerned the movements of the Takeda forces. He then secretly descended from the mountain under the cover of night, crossing the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川|qq=draft|qq=draft}} via the {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to outflank his enemy. According to legend, Uesugi’s troops performed {{ill|Sarugaku|ja|猿楽|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on an ancient Kofun at the summit, and he lodged at {{ill|Ruriji Temple|ja|瑠璃寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a temple along the gentle western slope toward {{ill|Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|土口将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The temple was later burned down by the Takeda forces after the battle. Currently, the location labeled as "Saijo-san" on maps—where an observation deck and battle explanation panels are installed—is actually a ridge summit of Saijo-zan (historically called "Akasaka" or "Akasaka-yama"). This site was where {{ill|Amakasu Kagemochi|ja|甘粕景持|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a rearguard commander in Uesugi’s army, set up his encampment. The site also contains a memorial hall and a monument honoring 960 war dead from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}} onward. This was built because, during the late Edo period, {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} warriors used the area for firearms training, leading to the Matsushiro lords establishing a shrine. Since the Meiji era, this site became a location for large-scale spring and autumn festivals honoring those who contributed to the establishment of the new government. As a result, "going to Saijo-san" became synonymous with visiting this location. However, the {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} worship hall was demolished in December 2023. == Naming Controversy == The modern name "Saijo-san" (妻女山, pronounced "Saijozan") became widespread from Edo-period dramas, {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kodan|ja|講談|qq=draft|qq=draft}} narratives, which romanticized the story that Uesugi’s warriors gazed northward across Kawanakajima, longing for their distant homeland and mourning their wives and families left behind. Historical records such as the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' refer to the encampment site as "Saijo-yama" (西條山). In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (ruled by the {{ill|Sanada clan|ja|真田氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) criticized this as an error, stating in the ''Shinbu Naiden'' (真武内伝) that "Saijo-yama is not Saijo-san; they are different mountains." Nevertheless, Edo-period maps continued to use the name "Saijo-yama" or "Saijo-san." In reality, the true "Saijo-yama" lies about 10 km to the south. It was used as a signal tower where the Takeda forces sent emergency fire signals to Kai Province when the Uesugi army invaded. It remains unclear why the Matsushiro Domain equated Saijo-yama with Saijo-san. However, some local theories, even today, suggest that Uesugi's true encampment was at "Saijo-zan" (斎場山), a site that contains numerous ancient tombs and was historically used for rituals honoring the dead. Saijo-zan is part of the Togami Mountain Range (also called Kurahone Mountain Range or Omine Mountain Range). It features two ancient fortresses: the upper Kurahone Castle (鞍骨城) and the lower Amagi Castle (天城城, also known as Irayama Castle). The ridge extends east-west, with its highest point being identified as Saijo-zan. However, some maps list the area as "Akasaka" (赤坂) and label "Saijo-san" instead, effectively erasing the original name of the main peak. Some theories support retaining the name "Saijo-san." According to local legend, the Kofun at the peak of Saijo-zan is the grave of {{ill|Aizu-hime|ja|会津比売命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, enshrined at the nearby {{ill|Aizu-hime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Locals also refer to the peak as "Tenjo" (天上) or "Otenjo" (御天上). Aizu-hime is believed to be the granddaughter of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the wife of {{ill|Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the legendary first {{ill|Governor of Shinano|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who is associated with the {{ill|Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This theory argues that the name "Saijo-san" is justified in this context. == Alternative Historical Interpretation == While it is commonly believed that Uesugi Kenshin encamped on Saijo-san, some scholars argue that there is no historical evidence for this. They propose that it is a case of mistaken history, confused with a later military action. In March {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 10 (1582), after {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s conquest of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Mori Nagayoshi|ja|森長可|qq=draft|qq=draft}} briefly occupied northern Shinano but withdrew following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In the ensuing {{ill|Tenshō Jingo War|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as {{ill|Hōjō Ujimasa|ja|北条氏直|qq=draft|qq=draft}} advanced northward, {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|ja|上杉景勝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} moved from {{ill|Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|ja|長沼城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to Kaizu Castle, establishing defensive positions at Saijo-san, Kurahone Castle, and Akazawa-yama. This forced the Hōjō forces to retreat and negotiate peace. Some historians argue that later records mistakenly conflated this event with the Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima. == Access and Wildlife == A paved road leads up to Akasaka Observation Deck, making it accessible by regular vehicles. Along the way, visitors may encounter {{ill|Japanese serow|ja|ニホンカモシカ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and tree trunks bear marks from wild {{ill|boar|ja|猪|qq=draft|qq=draft}} rubbing against them. While an unpaved road continues toward Saijo-san’s summit, it is difficult to traverse without a 4WD vehicle. During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizuhime no Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) - According to local legend, the Kofun at the peak of Saijo-zan is the grave of {{ill|Aizu-hime|ja|会津比売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime|qq=}}, enshrined at the nearby {{ill|Aizu-hime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime Shrine|qq=}}. Locals also refer to the peak as "Tenjo" (天上) or "Otenjo" (御天上). Aizu-hime is believed to be the granddaughter of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata|qq=}} and the wife of {{ill|Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|qq=}}, the legendary first {{ill|Governor of Shinano|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Shinano|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, who is associated with the {{ill|Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|qq=}}. This theory argues that the name "Saijo-san" is justified in this context. == Alternative Historical Interpretation == While it is commonly believed that Uesugi Kenshin encamped on Saijo-san, some scholars argue that there is no historical evidence for this. They propose that it is a case of mistaken history, confused with a later military action. In March {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō (Momoyama period)|qq=}} 10 (1582), after {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oda Nobunaga|qq=}}'s conquest of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda clan|qq=}}, {{ill|Mori Nagayoshi|ja|森長可|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Nagayoshi|qq=}} briefly occupied northern Shinano but withdrew following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honnō-ji Incident|qq=}}. In the ensuing {{ill|Tenshō Jingo War|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Jingo War|qq=}}, as {{ill|Hōjō Ujimasa|ja|北条氏直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōjō Ujimasa|qq=}} advanced northward, {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|ja|上杉景勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uesugi Kagekatsu|qq=}} moved from {{ill|Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|ja|長沼城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|qq=}} to Kaizu Castle, establishing defensive positions at Saijo-san, Kurahone Castle, and Akazawa-yama. This forced the Hōjō forces to retreat and negotiate peace. Some historians argue that later records mistakenly conflated this event with the Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima. == Access and Wildlife == A paved road leads up to Akasaka Observation Deck, making it accessible by regular vehicles. Along the way, visitors may encounter {{ill|Japanese serow|ja|ニホンカモシカ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese serow|qq=}}, and tree trunks bear marks from wild {{ill|boar|ja|猪|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boar|qq=}} rubbing against them. While an unpaved road continues toward Saijo-san’s summit, it is difficult to traverse without a 4WD vehicle. During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemiya no Watashi|qq=}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Shinshiro|qq=}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama-juku|qq=}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arato Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami-yama|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizuhime no Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) -
