{{Infobox mountain | Name = Saijo-san (妻女山) | image = [[Image:Saijyosan 1.jpg|300px|Saijo-san]] | image Caption = View of Saijo-san (Akasaka-yama) from the direction of [[Iwano Station]] | Elevation = 411 m | Coordinates = {{Coord|36|33|40.7|N|138|10|17.2|E|type:mountain_region:JP-20|display=inline,title}} | Location = {{JPN}}
{{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagano Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Nagano City|ja|長野市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagano City|qq=}} | Mountain Range = | Type = | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|36.5613|138.1714}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Saijo-san }} '''Saijo-san''' (妻女山) is a mountain located on the border between {{ill|Matsushiro, Nagano|ja|松代町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushiro, Nagano|qq=}} in {{ill|Nagano City|ja|長野市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagano City|qq=}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagano Prefecture|qq=}}, and {{ill|Chikuma City|ja|千曲市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikuma City|qq=}}, Tsuchiguchi. It is best known as the location where {{ill|Uesugi Kenshin|ja|上杉謙信|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uesugi Kenshin|qq=}} established his encampment during the {{ill|Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima|ja|川中島の戦い|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima|qq=}}. However, alternative theories suggest the true encampment was on '''Saijo-zan''' (斎場山). == Overview == Saijo-san is situated in a strategic location overlooking {{ill|Kawanakajima|ja|川中島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawanakajima|qq=}} and {{ill|Kaizu Castle|ja|海津城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizu Castle|qq=}}, which was built by {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|qq=}}. It is said that Uesugi Kenshin observed the rising smoke from Kaizu Castle’s many cooking fires and discerned the movements of the Takeda forces. He then secretly descended from the mountain under the cover of night, crossing the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikuma River|qq=}} via the {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemiya no Watashi|qq=}} to outflank his enemy. According to legend, Uesugi’s troops performed {{ill|Sarugaku|ja|猿楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarugaku|qq=}} on an ancient Kofun at the summit, and he lodged at {{ill|Ruriji Temple|ja|瑠璃寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ruriji Temple|qq=}}, a temple along the gentle western slope toward {{ill|Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|土口将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|qq=}}. The temple was later burned down by the Takeda forces after the battle. Currently, the location labeled as "Saijo-san" on maps—where an observation deck and battle explanation panels are installed—is actually a ridge summit of Saijo-zan (historically called "Akasaka" or "Akasaka-yama"). This site was where {{ill|Amakasu Kagemochi|ja|甘粕景持|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amakasu Kagemochi|qq=}}, a rearguard commander in Uesugi’s army, set up his encampment. The site also contains a memorial hall and a monument honoring 960 war dead from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boshin War|qq=}} onward. This was built because, during the late Edo period, {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushiro Domain|qq=}} warriors used the area for firearms training, leading to the Matsushiro lords establishing a shrine. Since the Meiji era, this site became a location for large-scale spring and autumn festivals honoring those who contributed to the establishment of the new government. As a result, "going to Saijo-san" became synonymous with visiting this location. However, the {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|qq=}} worship hall was demolished in December 2023. == Naming Controversy == The modern name "Saijo-san" (妻女山, pronounced "Saijozan") became widespread from Edo-period dramas, {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōruri|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kodan|ja|講談|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kodan|qq=}} narratives, which romanticized the story that Uesugi’s warriors gazed northward across Kawanakajima, longing for their distant homeland and mourning their wives and families left behind. Historical records such as the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōyō Gunkan|qq=}}'' refer to the encampment site as "Saijo-yama" (西條山). In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushiro Domain|qq=}} (ruled by the {{ill|Sanada clan|ja|真田氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanada clan|qq=}}) criticized this as an error, stating in the ''Shinbu Naiden'' (真武内伝) that "Saijo-yama is not Saijo-san; they are different mountains." Nevertheless, Edo-period maps continued to use the name "Saijo-yama" or "Saijo-san." In reality, the true "Saijo-yama" lies about 10 km to the south. It was used as a signal tower where the Takeda forces sent emergency fire signals to Kai Province when the Uesugi army invaded. It remains unclear why the Matsushiro Domain equated Saijo-yama with Saijo-san. However, some local theories, even today, suggest that Uesugi's true encampment was at "Saijo-zan" (斎場山), a site that contains numerous ancient tombs and was historically used for rituals honoring the dead. Saijo-zan is part of the Togami Mountain Range (also called Kurahone Mountain Range or Omine Mountain Range). It features two ancient fortresses: the upper Kurahone Castle (鞍骨城) and the lower Amagi Castle (天城城, also known as Irayama Castle). The ridge extends east-west, with its highest point being identified as Saijo-zan. However, some maps list the area as "Akasaka" (赤坂) and label "Saijo-san" instead, effectively erasing the original name of the main peak. Some theories support retaining the name "Saijo-san." According to local legend, the Kofun at the peak of Saijo-zan is the grave of {{ill|Aizu-hime|ja|会津比売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime|qq=}}, enshrined at the nearby {{ill|Aizu-hime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime Shrine|qq=}}. Locals also refer to the peak as "Tenjo" (天上) or "Otenjo" (御天上). Aizu-hime is believed to be the granddaughter of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata|qq=}} and the wife of {{ill|Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|qq=}}, the legendary first {{ill|Governor of Shinano|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Shinano|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, who is associated with the {{ill|Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|qq=}}. This theory argues that the name "Saijo-san" is justified in this context. == Alternative Historical Interpretation == While it is commonly believed that Uesugi Kenshin encamped on Saijo-san, some scholars argue that there is no historical evidence for this. They propose that it is a case of mistaken history, confused with a later military action. In March {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō (Momoyama period)|qq=}} 10 (1582), after {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oda Nobunaga|qq=}}'s conquest of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda clan|qq=}}, {{ill|Mori Nagayoshi|ja|森長可|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Nagayoshi|qq=}} briefly occupied northern Shinano but withdrew following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honnō-ji Incident|qq=}}. In the ensuing {{ill|Tenshō Jingo War|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Jingo War|qq=}}, as {{ill|Hōjō Ujimasa|ja|北条氏直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōjō Ujimasa|qq=}} advanced northward, {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|ja|上杉景勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uesugi Kagekatsu|qq=}} moved from {{ill|Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|ja|長沼城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|qq=}} to Kaizu Castle, establishing defensive positions at Saijo-san, Kurahone Castle, and Akazawa-yama. This forced the Hōjō forces to retreat and negotiate peace. Some historians argue that later records mistakenly conflated this event with the Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima. == Access and Wildlife == A paved road leads up to Akasaka Observation Deck, making it accessible by regular vehicles. Along the way, visitors may encounter {{ill|Japanese serow|ja|ニホンカモシカ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese serow|qq=}}, and tree trunks bear marks from wild {{ill|boar|ja|猪|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boar|qq=}} rubbing against them. While an unpaved road continues toward Saijo-san’s summit, it is difficult to traverse without a 4WD vehicle. During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemiya no Watashi|qq=}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Shinshiro|qq=}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama-juku|qq=}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arato Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami-yama|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizu-hime-no-Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) - {{Infobox mountain | Name = Saijo-san (妻女山) | image = [[Image:Saijyosan 1.jpg|300px|Saijo-san]] | image Caption = View of Saijo-san (Akasaka-yama) from the direction of [[Iwano Station]] | Elevation = 411 m | Coordinates = {{Coord|36|33|40.7|N|138|10|17.2|E|type:mountain_region:JP-20|display=inline,title}} | Location = {{JPN}}
{{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagano Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Nagano City|ja|長野市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagano City|qq=}} | Mountain Range = | Type = | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|36.5613|138.1714}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Saijo-san }} '''Saijo-san''' (妻女山) is a mountain located on the border between {{ill|Matsushiro, Nagano|ja|松代町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushiro, Nagano|qq=}} in {{ill|Nagano City|ja|長野市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagano City|qq=}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagano Prefecture|qq=}}, and {{ill|Chikuma City|ja|千曲市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikuma City|qq=}}, Tsuchiguchi. It is best known as the location where {{ill|Uesugi Kenshin|ja|上杉謙信|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uesugi Kenshin|qq=}} established his encampment during the {{ill|Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima|ja|川中島の戦い|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima|qq=}}. However, alternative theories suggest the true encampment was on '''Saijo-zan''' (斎場山). == Overview == Saijo-san is situated in a strategic location overlooking {{ill|Kawanakajima|ja|川中島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawanakajima|qq=}} and {{ill|Kaizu Castle|ja|海津城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizu Castle|qq=}}, which was built by {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|qq=}}. It is said that Uesugi Kenshin observed the rising smoke from Kaizu Castle’s many cooking fires and discerned the movements of the Takeda forces. He then secretly descended from the mountain under the cover of night, crossing the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikuma River|qq=}} via the {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemiya no Watashi|qq=}} to outflank his enemy. According to legend, Uesugi’s troops performed {{ill|Sarugaku|ja|猿楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarugaku|qq=}} on an ancient Kofun at the summit, and he lodged at {{ill|Ruriji Temple|ja|瑠璃寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ruriji Temple|qq=}}, a temple along the gentle western slope toward {{ill|Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|土口将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|qq=}}. The temple was later burned down by the Takeda forces after the battle. Currently, the location labeled as "Saijo-san" on maps—where an observation deck and battle explanation panels are installed—is actually a ridge summit of Saijo-zan (historically called "Akasaka" or "Akasaka-yama"). This site was where {{ill|Amakasu Kagemochi|ja|甘粕景持|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amakasu Kagemochi|qq=}}, a rearguard commander in Uesugi’s army, set up his encampment. The site also contains a memorial hall and a monument honoring 960 war dead from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boshin War|qq=}} onward. This was built because, during the late Edo period, {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushiro Domain|qq=}} warriors used the area for firearms training, leading to the Matsushiro lords establishing a shrine. Since the Meiji era, this site became a location for large-scale spring and autumn festivals honoring those who contributed to the establishment of the new government. As a result, "going to Saijo-san" became synonymous with visiting this location. However, the {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|qq=}} worship hall was demolished in December 2023. == Naming Controversy == The modern name "Saijo-san" (妻女山, pronounced "Saijozan") became widespread from Edo-period dramas, {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōruri|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kodan|ja|講談|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kodan|qq=}} narratives, which romanticized the story that Uesugi’s warriors gazed northward across Kawanakajima, longing for their distant homeland and mourning their wives and families left behind. Historical records such as the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōyō Gunkan|qq=}}'' refer to the encampment site as "Saijo-yama" (西條山). In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushiro Domain|qq=}} (ruled by the {{ill|Sanada clan|ja|真田氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanada clan|qq=}}) criticized this as an error, stating in the ''Shinbu Naiden'' (真武内伝) that "Saijo-yama is not Saijo-san; they are different mountains." Nevertheless, Edo-period maps continued to use the name "Saijo-yama" or "Saijo-san." In reality, the true "Saijo-yama" lies about 10 km to the south. It was used as a signal tower where the Takeda forces sent emergency fire signals to Kai Province when the Uesugi army invaded. It remains unclear why the Matsushiro Domain equated Saijo-yama with Saijo-san. However, some local theories, even today, suggest that Uesugi's true encampment was at "Saijo-zan" (斎場山), a site that contains numerous ancient tombs and was historically used for rituals honoring the dead. Saijo-zan is part of the Togami Mountain Range (also called Kurahone Mountain Range or Omine Mountain Range). It features two ancient fortresses: the upper Kurahone Castle (鞍骨城) and the lower Amagi Castle (天城城, also known as Irayama Castle). The ridge extends east-west, with its highest point being identified as Saijo-zan. However, some maps list the area as "Akasaka" (赤坂) and label "Saijo-san" instead, effectively erasing the original name of the main peak. Some theories support retaining the name "Saijo-san." According to local legend, the Kofun at the peak of Saijo-zan is the grave of {{ill|Aizu-hime|ja|会津比売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime|qq=}}, enshrined at the nearby {{ill|Aizu-hime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime Shrine|qq=}}. Locals also refer to the peak as "Tenjo" (天上) or "Otenjo" (御天上). Aizu-hime is believed to be the granddaughter of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata|qq=}} and the wife of {{ill|Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|qq=}}, the legendary first {{ill|Governor of Shinano|ja|科野国造|1=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Shinano|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, who is associated with the {{ill|Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|qq=}}. This theory argues that the name "Saijo-san" is justified in this context. == Alternative Historical Interpretation == While it is commonly believed that Uesugi Kenshin encamped on Saijo-san, some scholars argue that there is no historical evidence for this. They propose that it is a case of mistaken history, confused with a later military action. In March {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō (Momoyama period)|qq=}} 10 (1582), after {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oda Nobunaga|qq=}}'s conquest of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda clan|qq=}}, {{ill|Mori Nagayoshi|ja|森長可|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Nagayoshi|qq=}} briefly occupied northern Shinano but withdrew following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honnō-ji Incident|qq=}}. In the ensuing {{ill|Tenshō Jingo War|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Jingo War|qq=}}, as {{ill|Hōjō Ujimasa|ja|北条氏直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōjō Ujimasa|qq=}} advanced northward, {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|ja|上杉景勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uesugi Kagekatsu|qq=}} moved from {{ill|Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|ja|長沼城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|qq=}} to Kaizu Castle, establishing defensive positions at Saijo-san, Kurahone Castle, and Akazawa-yama. This forced the Hōjō forces to retreat and negotiate peace. Some historians argue that later records mistakenly conflated this event with the Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima. == Access and Wildlife == A paved road leads up to Akasaka Observation Deck, making it accessible by regular vehicles. Along the way, visitors may encounter {{ill|Japanese serow|ja|ニホンカモシカ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese serow|qq=}}, and tree trunks bear marks from wild {{ill|boar|ja|猪|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boar|qq=}} rubbing against them. While an unpaved road continues toward Saijo-san’s summit, it is difficult to traverse without a 4WD vehicle. During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemiya no Watashi|qq=}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Shinshiro|qq=}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama-juku|qq=}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arato Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Mount Minakami|lt=Mount Minakami|lt=Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami-yama|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizu-hime-no-Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) - This site was where {{ill|Amakasu Kagemochi|ja|甘粕景持|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amakasu Kagemochi|qq=}}, a rearguard commander in Uesugi’s army, set up his encampment. The site also contains a memorial hall and a monument honoring 960 war dead from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boshin War|qq=}} onward. This was built because, during the late Edo period, {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushiro Domain|qq=}} warriors used the area for firearms training, leading to the Matsushiro lords establishing a shrine. Since the Meiji era, this site became a location for large-scale spring and autumn festivals honoring those who contributed to the establishment of the new government. As a result, "going to Saijo-san" became synonymous with visiting this location. However, the {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|qq=}} worship hall was demolished in December 2023. == Naming Controversy == The modern name "Saijo-san" (妻女山, pronounced "Saijozan") became widespread from Edo-period dramas, {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōruri|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kodan|ja|講談|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kodan|qq=}} narratives, which romanticized the story that Uesugi’s warriors gazed northward across Kawanakajima, longing for their distant homeland and mourning their wives and families left behind. Historical records such as the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōyō Gunkan|qq=}}'' refer to the encampment site as "Saijo-yama" (西條山). In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushiro Domain|qq=}} (ruled by the {{ill|Sanada clan|ja|真田氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanada clan|qq=}}) criticized this as an error, stating in the ''Shinbu Naiden'' (真武内伝) that "Saijo-yama is not Saijo-san; they are different mountains." Nevertheless, Edo-period maps continued to use the name "Saijo-yama" or "Saijo-san." In reality, the true "Saijo-yama" lies about 10 km to the south. It was used as a signal tower where the Takeda forces sent emergency fire signals to Kai Province when the Uesugi army invaded. It remains unclear why the Matsushiro Domain equated Saijo-yama with Saijo-san. However, some local theories, even today, suggest that Uesugi's true encampment was at "Saijo-zan" (斎場山), a site that contains numerous ancient tombs and was historically used for rituals honoring the dead. Saijo-zan is part of the Togami Mountain Range (also called Kurahone Mountain Range or Omine Mountain Range). It features two ancient fortresses: the upper Kurahone Castle (鞍骨城) and the lower Amagi Castle (天城城, also known as Irayama Castle). The ridge extends east-west, with its highest point being identified as Saijo-zan. However, some maps list the area as "Akasaka" (赤坂) and label "Saijo-san" instead, effectively erasing the original name of the main peak. Some theories support retaining the name "Saijo-san." According to local legend, the Kofun at the peak of Saijo-zan is the grave of {{ill|Aizu-hime|ja|会津比売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime|qq=}}, enshrined at the nearby {{ill|Aizu-hime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime Shrine|qq=}}. Locals also refer to the peak as "Tenjo" (天上) or "Otenjo" (御天上). Aizu-hime is believed to be the granddaughter of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata|qq=}} and the wife of {{ill|Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|qq=}}, the legendary first {{ill|Governor of Shinano|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Shinano|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, who is associated with the {{ill|Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|qq=}}. This theory argues that the name "Saijo-san" is justified in this context. == Alternative Historical Interpretation == While it is commonly believed that Uesugi Kenshin encamped on Saijo-san, some scholars argue that there is no historical evidence for this. They propose that it is a case of mistaken history, confused with a later military action. In March {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō (Momoyama period)|qq=}} 10 (1582), after {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oda Nobunaga|qq=}}'s conquest of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda clan|qq=}}, {{ill|Mori Nagayoshi|ja|森長可|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Nagayoshi|qq=}} briefly occupied northern Shinano but withdrew following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honnō-ji Incident|qq=}}. In the ensuing {{ill|Tenshō Jingo War|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Jingo War|qq=}}, as {{ill|Hōjō Ujimasa|ja|北条氏直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōjō Ujimasa|qq=}} advanced northward, {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|ja|上杉景勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uesugi Kagekatsu|qq=}} moved from {{ill|Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|ja|長沼城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|qq=}} to Kaizu Castle, establishing defensive positions at Saijo-san, Kurahone Castle, and Akazawa-yama. This forced the Hōjō forces to retreat and negotiate peace. Some historians argue that later records mistakenly conflated this event with the Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima. == Access and Wildlife == A paved road leads up to Akasaka Observation Deck, making it accessible by regular vehicles. Along the way, visitors may encounter {{ill|Japanese serow|ja|ニホンカモシカ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese serow|qq=}}, and tree trunks bear marks from wild {{ill|boar|ja|猪|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boar|qq=}} rubbing against them. While an unpaved road continues toward Saijo-san’s summit, it is difficult to traverse without a 4WD vehicle. During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemiya no Watashi|qq=}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Shinshiro|qq=}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama-juku|qq=}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arato Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Mount Minakami|lt=Mount Minakami|lt=Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami-yama|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizu-hime-no-Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) - revision.text:
{{Infobox mountain | Name = Saijo-san (妻女山) | image = [[Image:Saijyosan 1.jpg|300px|Saijo-san]] | image Caption = View of Saijo-san (Akasaka-yama) from the direction of [[Iwano Station]] | Elevation = 411 m | Coordinates = {{Coord|36|33|40.7|N|138|10|17.2|E|type:mountain_region:JP-20|display=inline,title}} | Location = {{JPN}}
{{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagano Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Nagano City|ja|長野市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagano City|qq=}} | Mountain Range = | Type = | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|36.5613|138.1714}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Saijo-san }} '''Saijo-san''' (妻女山) is a mountain located on the border between {{ill|Matsushiro, Nagano|ja|松代町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushiro, Nagano|qq=}} in {{ill|Nagano City|ja|長野市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagano City|qq=}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagano Prefecture|qq=}}, and {{ill|Chikuma City|ja|千曲市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikuma City|qq=}}, Tsuchiguchi. It is best known as the location where {{ill|Uesugi Kenshin|ja|上杉謙信|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uesugi Kenshin|qq=}} established his encampment during the {{ill|Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima|ja|川中島の戦い|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima|qq=}}. However, alternative theories suggest the true encampment was on '''Saijo-zan''' (斎場山). == Overview == Saijo-san is situated in a strategic location overlooking {{ill|Kawanakajima|ja|川中島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawanakajima|qq=}} and {{ill|Kaizu Castle|ja|海津城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizu Castle|qq=}}, which was built by {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|qq=}}. It is said that Uesugi Kenshin observed the rising smoke from Kaizu Castle’s many cooking fires and discerned the movements of the Takeda forces. He then secretly descended from the mountain under the cover of night, crossing the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikuma River|qq=}} via the {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemiya no Watashi|qq=}} to outflank his enemy. According to legend, Uesugi’s troops performed {{ill|Sarugaku|ja|猿楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarugaku|qq=}} on an ancient Kofun at the summit, and he lodged at {{ill|Ruriji Temple|ja|瑠璃寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ruriji Temple|qq=}}, a temple along the gentle western slope toward {{ill|Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|土口将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|qq=}}. The temple was later burned down by the Takeda forces after the battle. Currently, the location labeled as "Saijo-san" on maps—where an observation deck and battle explanation panels are installed—is actually a ridge summit of Saijo-zan (historically called "Akasaka" or "Akasaka-yama"). This site was where {{ill|Amakasu Kagemochi|ja|甘粕景持|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amakasu Kagemochi|qq=}}, a rearguard commander in Uesugi’s army, set up his encampment. The site also contains a memorial hall and a monument honoring 960 war dead from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boshin War|qq=}} onward. This was built because, during the late Edo period, {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushiro Domain|qq=}} warriors used the area for firearms training, leading to the Matsushiro lords establishing a shrine. Since the Meiji era, this site became a location for large-scale spring and autumn festivals honoring those who contributed to the establishment of the new government. As a result, "going to Saijo-san" became synonymous with visiting this location. However, the {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|qq=}} worship hall was demolished in December 2023. == Naming Controversy == The modern name "Saijo-san" (妻女山, pronounced "Saijozan") became widespread from Edo-period dramas, {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōruri|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kodan|ja|講談|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kodan|qq=}} narratives, which romanticized the story that Uesugi’s warriors gazed northward across Kawanakajima, longing for their distant homeland and mourning their wives and families left behind. Historical records such as the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōyō Gunkan|qq=}}'' refer to the encampment site as "Saijo-yama" (西條山). In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushiro Domain|qq=}} (ruled by the {{ill|Sanada clan|ja|真田氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanada clan|qq=}}) criticized this as an error, stating in the ''Shinbu Naiden'' (真武内伝) that "Saijo-yama is not Saijo-san; they are different mountains." Nevertheless, Edo-period maps continued to use the name "Saijo-yama" or "Saijo-san." In reality, the true "Saijo-yama" lies about 10 km to the south. It was used as a signal tower where the Takeda forces sent emergency fire signals to Kai Province when the Uesugi army invaded. It remains unclear why the Matsushiro Domain equated Saijo-yama with Saijo-san. However, some local theories, even today, suggest that Uesugi's true encampment was at "Saijo-zan" (斎場山), a site that contains numerous ancient tombs and was historically used for rituals honoring the dead. Saijo-zan is part of the Togami Mountain Range (also called Kurahone Mountain Range or Omine Mountain Range). It features two ancient fortresses: the upper Kurahone Castle (鞍骨城) and the lower Amagi Castle (天城城, also known as Irayama Castle). The ridge extends east-west, with its highest point being identified as Saijo-zan. However, some maps list the area as "Akasaka" (赤坂) and label "Saijo-san" instead, effectively erasing the original name of the main peak. Some theories support retaining the name "Saijo-san." According to local legend, the Kofun at the peak of Saijo-zan is the grave of {{ill|Aizu-hime|ja|会津比売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime|qq=}}, enshrined at the nearby {{ill|Aizu-hime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime Shrine|qq=}}. Locals also refer to the peak as "Tenjo" (天上) or "Otenjo" (御天上). Aizu-hime is believed to be the granddaughter of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神|1=Takeminakata|2=ja|3=タケミナカタ|lt=Takeminakata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata|qq=}} and the wife of {{ill|Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|qq=}}, the legendary first {{ill|Governor of Shinano|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Shinano|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, who is associated with the {{ill|Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|qq=}}. This theory argues that the name "Saijo-san" is justified in this context. == Alternative Historical Interpretation == While it is commonly believed that Uesugi Kenshin encamped on Saijo-san, some scholars argue that there is no historical evidence for this. They propose that it is a case of mistaken history, confused with a later military action. In March {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō (Momoyama period)|qq=}} 10 (1582), after {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oda Nobunaga|qq=}}'s conquest of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda clan|qq=}}, {{ill|Mori Nagayoshi|ja|森長可|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Nagayoshi|qq=}} briefly occupied northern Shinano but withdrew following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honnō-ji Incident|qq=}}. Along the way, visitors may encounter {{ill|Japanese serow|ja|ニホンカモシカ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese serow|qq=}}, and tree trunks bear marks from wild {{ill|boar|ja|猪|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boar|qq=}} rubbing against them. While an unpaved road continues toward Saijo-san’s summit, it is difficult to traverse without a 4WD vehicle. During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemiya no Watashi|qq=}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Shinshiro|qq=}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama-juku|qq=}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arato Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Mount Minakami|lt=Mount Minakami|lt=Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami-yama|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizu-hime-no-Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) - revision.text:
{{Infobox mountain | Name = Saijo-san (妻女山) | image = [[Image:Saijyosan 1.jpg|300px|Saijo-san]] | image Caption = View of Saijo-san (Akasaka-yama) from the direction of [[Iwano Station]] | Elevation = 411 m | Coordinates = {{Coord|36|33|40.7|N|138|10|17.2|E|type:mountain_region:JP-20|display=inline,title}} | Location = {{JPN}}
[[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]] | Mountain Range = | Type = | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|36.5613|138.1714}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Saijo-san }} '''Saijo-san''' (妻女山) is a mountain located on the border between {{ill|Matsushiro, Nagano|ja|松代町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushiro, Nagano|qq=}} in [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], and [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma City]], Tsuchiguchi. It is best known as the location where [[:en:Uesugi Kenshin|Uesugi Kenshin]] established his encampment during the [[:en:Battles of Kawanakajima|Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima]]. However, alternative theories suggest the true encampment was on '''Saijo-zan''' (斎場山). == Overview == Saijo-san is situated in a strategic location overlooking {{ill|Kawanakajima|ja|川中島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawanakajima|qq=}} and {{ill|Kaizu Castle|ja|海津城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizu Castle|qq=}}, which was built by {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|qq=}}. It is said that Uesugi Kenshin observed the rising smoke from Kaizu Castle’s many cooking fires and discerned the movements of the Takeda forces. He then secretly descended from the mountain under the cover of night, crossing the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikuma River|qq=}} via the {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemiya no Watashi|qq=}} to outflank his enemy. According to legend, Uesugi’s troops performed [[:en:Sarugaku|Sarugaku]] on an ancient Kofun at the summit, and he lodged at {{ill|Ruriji Temple|ja|瑠璃寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ruriji Temple|qq=}}, a temple along the gentle western slope toward {{ill|Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|土口将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|qq=}}. The temple was later burned down by the Takeda forces after the battle. Currently, the location labeled as "Saijo-san" on maps—where an observation deck and battle explanation panels are installed—is actually a ridge summit of Saijo-zan (historically called "Akasaka" or "Akasaka-yama"). This site was where [[:en:Amakasu Kagemochi|Amakasu Kagemochi]], a rearguard commander in Uesugi’s army, set up his encampment. The site also contains a memorial hall and a monument honoring 960 war dead from the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]] onward. This was built because, during the late Edo period, [[:en:Matsushiro Domain|Matsushiro Domain]] warriors used the area for firearms training, leading to the Matsushiro lords establishing a shrine. Since the Meiji era, this site became a location for large-scale spring and autumn festivals honoring those who contributed to the establishment of the new government. As a result, "going to Saijo-san" became synonymous with visiting this location. However, the {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|qq=}} worship hall was demolished in December 2023. == Naming Controversy == The modern name "Saijo-san" (妻女山, pronounced "Saijozan") became widespread from Edo-period dramas, [[:en:Jōruri (music)|Jōruri]], and [[:en:Kōdan|Kodan]] narratives, which romanticized the story that Uesugi’s warriors gazed northward across Kawanakajima, longing for their distant homeland and mourning their wives and families left behind. Historical records such as the ''[[:en:Kōyō Gunkan|Kōyō Gunkan]]'' refer to the encampment site as "Saijo-yama" (西條山). In the mid-Edo period, the [[:en:Matsushiro Domain|Matsushiro Domain]] (ruled by the [[:en:Sanada clan|Sanada clan]]) criticized this as an error, stating in the ''Shinbu Naiden'' (真武内伝) that "Saijo-yama is not Saijo-san; they are different mountains." Nevertheless, Edo-period maps continued to use the name "Saijo-yama" or "Saijo-san." In reality, the true "Saijo-yama" lies about 10 km to the south. It was used as a signal tower where the Takeda forces sent emergency fire signals to Kai Province when the Uesugi army invaded. It remains unclear why the Matsushiro Domain equated Saijo-yama with Saijo-san. However, some local theories, even today, suggest that Uesugi's true encampment was at "Saijo-zan" (斎場山), a site that contains numerous ancient tombs and was historically used for rituals honoring the dead. Saijo-zan is part of the Togami Mountain Range (also called Kurahone Mountain Range or Omine Mountain Range). It features two ancient fortresses: the upper Kurahone Castle (鞍骨城) and the lower Amagi Castle (天城城, also known as Irayama Castle). The ridge extends east-west, with its highest point being identified as Saijo-zan. However, some maps list the area as "Akasaka" (赤坂) and label "Saijo-san" instead, effectively erasing the original name of the main peak. Some theories support retaining the name "Saijo-san." According to local legend, the Kofun at the peak of Saijo-zan is the grave of {{ill|Aizu-hime|ja|会津比売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime|qq=}}, enshrined at the nearby {{ill|Aizu-hime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime Shrine|qq=}}. Locals also refer to the peak as "Tenjo" (天上) or "Otenjo" (御天上). Aizu-hime is believed to be the granddaughter of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神|1=Takeminakata|2=ja|3=タケミナカタ|lt=Takeminakata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata|qq=}} and the wife of {{ill|Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|qq=}}, the legendary first {{ill|Governor of Shinano|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Shinano|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, who is associated with the {{ill|Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|qq=}}. This theory argues that the name "Saijo-san" is justified in this context. == Alternative Historical Interpretation == While it is commonly believed that Uesugi Kenshin encamped on Saijo-san, some scholars argue that there is no historical evidence for this. They propose that it is a case of mistaken history, confused with a later military action. In March [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 10 (1582), after [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]'s conquest of the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]], [[:en:Mori Nagayoshi|Mori Nagayoshi]] briefly occupied northern Shinano but withdrew following the [[:en:Honnō-ji Incident|Honnō-ji Incident]]. In the ensuing {{ill|Tenshō Jingo War|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Jingo War|qq=}}, as [[:en:Hōjō Ujinao|Hōjō Ujimasa]] advanced northward, [[:en:Uesugi Kagekatsu|Uesugi Kagekatsu]] moved from {{ill|Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|ja|長沼城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|qq=}} to Kaizu Castle, establishing defensive positions at Saijo-san, Kurahone Castle, and Akazawa-yama. This forced the Hōjō forces to retreat and negotiate peace. Some historians argue that later records mistakenly conflated this event with the Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima. == Access and Wildlife == A paved road leads up to Akasaka Observation Deck, making it accessible by regular vehicles. Along the way, visitors may encounter [[:en:Japanese serow|Japanese serow]], and tree trunks bear marks from wild {{ill|boar|ja|猪|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boar|qq=}} rubbing against them. While an unpaved road continues toward Saijo-san’s summit, it is difficult to traverse without a 4WD vehicle. During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemiya no Watashi|qq=}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Shinshiro|qq=}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama-juku|qq=}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arato Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Mount Minakami|lt=Mount Minakami|lt=Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami-yama|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizu-hime-no-Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) - [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]] | Mountain Range = | Type = | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|36.5613|138.1714}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Saijo-san }} '''Saijo-san''' (妻女山) is a mountain located on the border between {{ill|Matsushiro, Nagano|ja|松代町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushiro, Nagano|qq=}} in [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], and [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma City]], Tsuchiguchi. Currently, the location labeled as "Saijo-san" on maps—where an observation deck and battle explanation panels are installed—is actually a ridge summit of Saijo-zan (historically called "Akasaka" or "Akasaka-yama"). This site was where [[:en:Amakasu Kagemochi|Amakasu Kagemochi]], a rearguard commander in Uesugi’s army, set up his encampment. The site also contains a memorial hall and a monument honoring 960 war dead from the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]] onward. This was built because, during the late Edo period, [[:en:Matsushiro Domain|Matsushiro Domain]] warriors used the area for firearms training, leading to the Matsushiro lords establishing a shrine. Since the Meiji era, this site became a location for large-scale spring and autumn festivals honoring those who contributed to the establishment of the new government. As a result, "going to Saijo-san" became synonymous with visiting this location. However, the {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|qq=}} worship hall was demolished in December 2023. == Naming Controversy == The modern name "Saijo-san" (妻女山, pronounced "Saijozan") became widespread from Edo-period dramas, [[:en:Jōruri (music)|Jōruri]], and [[:en:Kōdan|Kodan]] narratives, which romanticized the story that Uesugi’s warriors gazed northward across Kawanakajima, longing for their distant homeland and mourning their wives and families left behind. Historical records such as the ''[[:en:Kōyō Gunkan|Kōyō Gunkan]]'' refer to the encampment site as "Saijo-yama" (西條山). In the mid-Edo period, the [[:en:Matsushiro Domain|Matsushiro Domain]] (ruled by the [[:en:Sanada clan|Sanada clan]]) criticized this as an error, stating in the ''Shinbu Naiden'' (真武内伝) that "Saijo-yama is not Saijo-san; they are different mountains." Nevertheless, Edo-period maps continued to use the name "Saijo-yama" or "Saijo-san." In reality, the true "Saijo-yama" lies about 10 km to the south. It was used as a signal tower where the Takeda forces sent emergency fire signals to Kai Province when the Uesugi army invaded. It remains unclear why the Matsushiro Domain equated Saijo-yama with Saijo-san. However, some local theories, even today, suggest that Uesugi's true encampment was at "Saijo-zan" (斎場山), a site that contains numerous ancient tombs and was historically used for rituals honoring the dead. Saijo-zan is part of the Togami Mountain Range (also called Kurahone Mountain Range or Omine Mountain Range). It features two ancient fortresses: the upper Kurahone Castle (鞍骨城) and the lower Amagi Castle (天城城, also known as Irayama Castle). The ridge extends east-west, with its highest point being identified as Saijo-zan. However, some maps list the area as "Akasaka" (赤坂) and label "Saijo-san" instead, effectively erasing the original name of the main peak. Some theories support retaining the name "Saijo-san." According to local legend, the Kofun at the peak of Saijo-zan is the grave of {{ill|Aizu-hime|ja|会津比売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime|qq=}}, enshrined at the nearby {{ill|Aizu-hime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime Shrine|qq=}}. Locals also refer to the peak as "Tenjo" (天上) or "Otenjo" (御天上). Aizu-hime is believed to be the granddaughter of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神|1=Takeminakata|2=ja|3=タケミナカタ|lt=Takeminakata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata|qq=}} and the wife of {{ill|Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|qq=}}, the legendary first {{ill|Governor of Shinano|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Shinano|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, who is associated with the {{ill|Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|qq=}}. This theory argues that the name "Saijo-san" is justified in this context. == Alternative Historical Interpretation == While it is commonly believed that Uesugi Kenshin encamped on Saijo-san, some scholars argue that there is no historical evidence for this. They propose that it is a case of mistaken history, confused with a later military action. In March [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 10 (1582), after [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]'s conquest of the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]], [[:en:Mori Nagayoshi|Mori Nagayoshi]] briefly occupied northern Shinano but withdrew following the [[:en:Honnō-ji Incident|Honnō-ji Incident]]. In the ensuing {{ill|Tenshō Jingo War|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Jingo War|qq=}}, as [[:en:Hōjō Ujinao|Hōjō Ujimasa]] advanced northward, [[:en:Uesugi Kagekatsu|Uesugi Kagekatsu]] moved from {{ill|Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|ja|長沼城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|qq=}} to Kaizu Castle, establishing defensive positions at Saijo-san, Kurahone Castle, and Akazawa-yama. This forced the Hōjō forces to retreat and negotiate peace. Some historians argue that later records mistakenly conflated this event with the Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima. == Access and Wildlife == A paved road leads up to Akasaka Observation Deck, making it accessible by regular vehicles. Along the way, visitors may encounter [[:en:Japanese serow|Japanese serow]], and tree trunks bear marks from wild {{ill|boar|ja|猪|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boar|qq=}} rubbing against them. While an unpaved road continues toward Saijo-san’s summit, it is difficult to traverse without a 4WD vehicle. During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemiya no Watashi|qq=}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Shinshiro|qq=}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama-juku|qq=}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arato Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Mount Minakami|lt=Mount Minakami|lt=Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami-yama|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizu-hime-no-Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) - {{Infobox mountain | Name = Saijo-san (妻女山) | image = [[Image:Saijyosan 1.jpg|300px|Saijo-san]] | image Caption = View of Saijo-san (Akasaka-yama) from the direction of [[Iwano Station]] | Elevation = 411 m | Coordinates = {{Coord|36|33|40.7|N|138|10|17.2|E|type:mountain_region:JP-20|display=inline,title}} | Location = {{JPN}}
[[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]] | Mountain Range = | Type = | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|36.5613|138.1714}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Saijo-san }} '''Saijo-san''' (妻女山) is a mountain located on the border between {{ill|Matsushiro, Nagano|ja|松代町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushiro, Nagano|qq=}} in [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], and [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma City]], Tsuchiguchi. It is best known as the location where [[:en:Uesugi Kenshin|Uesugi Kenshin]] established his encampment during the [[:en:Battles of Kawanakajima|Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima]]. However, alternative theories suggest the true encampment was on '''Saijo-zan''' (斎場山). == Overview == Saijo-san is situated in a strategic location overlooking {{ill|Kawanakajima|ja|川中島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawanakajima|qq=}} and {{ill|Kaizu Castle|ja|海津城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizu Castle|qq=}}, which was built by {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|qq=}}. It is said that Uesugi Kenshin observed the rising smoke from Kaizu Castle’s many cooking fires and discerned the movements of the Takeda forces. He then secretly descended from the mountain under the cover of night, crossing the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikuma River|qq=}} via the {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemiya no Watashi|qq=}} to outflank his enemy. According to legend, Uesugi’s troops performed [[:en:Sarugaku|Sarugaku]] on an ancient Kofun at the summit, and he lodged at {{ill|Ruriji Temple|ja|瑠璃寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ruriji Temple|qq=}}, a temple along the gentle western slope toward {{ill|Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|土口将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|qq=}}. The temple was later burned down by the Takeda forces after the battle. Currently, the location labeled as "Saijo-san" on maps—where an observation deck and battle explanation panels are installed—is actually a ridge summit of Saijo-zan (historically called "Akasaka" or "Akasaka-yama"). This site was where [[:en:Amakasu Kagemochi|Amakasu Kagemochi]], a rearguard commander in Uesugi’s army, set up his encampment. The site also contains a memorial hall and a monument honoring 960 war dead from the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]] onward. This was built because, during the late Edo period, [[:en:Matsushiro Domain|Matsushiro Domain]] warriors used the area for firearms training, leading to the Matsushiro lords establishing a shrine. Since the Meiji era, this site became a location for large-scale spring and autumn festivals honoring those who contributed to the establishment of the new government. As a result, "going to Saijo-san" became synonymous with visiting this location. However, the {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|qq=}} worship hall was demolished in December 2023. == Naming Controversy == The modern name "Saijo-san" (妻女山, pronounced "Saijozan") became widespread from Edo-period dramas, [[:en:Jōruri (music)|Jōruri]], and [[:en:Kōdan|Kodan]] narratives, which romanticized the story that Uesugi’s warriors gazed northward across Kawanakajima, longing for their distant homeland and mourning their wives and families left behind. Historical records such as the ''[[:en:Kōyō Gunkan|Kōyō Gunkan]]'' refer to the encampment site as "Saijo-yama" (西條山). In the mid-Edo period, the [[:en:Matsushiro Domain|Matsushiro Domain]] (ruled by the [[:en:Sanada clan|Sanada clan]]) criticized this as an error, stating in the ''Shinbu Naiden'' (真武内伝) that "Saijo-yama is not Saijo-san; they are different mountains." Nevertheless, Edo-period maps continued to use the name "Saijo-yama" or "Saijo-san." In reality, the true "Saijo-yama" lies about 10 km to the south. It was used as a signal tower where the Takeda forces sent emergency fire signals to Kai Province when the Uesugi army invaded. It remains unclear why the Matsushiro Domain equated Saijo-yama with Saijo-san. However, some local theories, even today, suggest that Uesugi's true encampment was at "Saijo-zan" (斎場山), a site that contains numerous ancient tombs and was historically used for rituals honoring the dead. Saijo-zan is part of the Togami Mountain Range (also called Kurahone Mountain Range or Omine Mountain Range). It features two ancient fortresses: the upper Kurahone Castle (鞍骨城) and the lower Amagi Castle (天城城, also known as Irayama Castle). The ridge extends east-west, with its highest point being identified as Saijo-zan. However, some maps list the area as "Akasaka" (赤坂) and label "Saijo-san" instead, effectively erasing the original name of the main peak. Some theories support retaining the name "Saijo-san." According to local legend, the Kofun at the peak of Saijo-zan is the grave of {{ill|Aizu-hime|ja|会津比売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime|qq=}}, enshrined at the nearby {{ill|Aizu-hime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime Shrine|qq=}}. Locals also refer to the peak as "Tenjo" (天上) or "Otenjo" (御天上). Aizu-hime is believed to be the granddaughter of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神|1=Takeminakata|2=ja|3=タケミナカタ|lt=Takeminakata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata|qq=}} and the wife of {{ill|Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|qq=}}, the legendary first {{ill|Governor of Shinano|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Shinano|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, who is associated with the {{ill|Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|qq=}}. This theory argues that the name "Saijo-san" is justified in this context. == Alternative Historical Interpretation == While it is commonly believed that Uesugi Kenshin encamped on Saijo-san, some scholars argue that there is no historical evidence for this. They propose that it is a case of mistaken history, confused with a later military action. In March [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 10 (1582), after [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]'s conquest of the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]], [[:en:Mori Nagayoshi|Mori Nagayoshi]] briefly occupied northern Shinano but withdrew following the [[:en:Honnō-ji Incident|Honnō-ji Incident]]. In the ensuing {{ill|Tenshō Jingo War|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Jingo War|qq=}}, as [[:en:Hōjō Ujinao|Hōjō Ujimasa]] advanced northward, [[:en:Uesugi Kagekatsu|Uesugi Kagekatsu]] moved from {{ill|Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|ja|長沼城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|qq=}} to Kaizu Castle, establishing defensive positions at Saijo-san, Kurahone Castle, and Akazawa-yama. This forced the Hōjō forces to retreat and negotiate peace. Some historians argue that later records mistakenly conflated this event with the Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima. == Access and Wildlife == A paved road leads up to Akasaka Observation Deck, making it accessible by regular vehicles. Along the way, visitors may encounter [[:en:Japanese serow|Japanese serow]], and tree trunks bear marks from wild {{ill|boar|ja|猪|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boar|qq=}} rubbing against them. While an unpaved road continues toward Saijo-san’s summit, it is difficult to traverse without a 4WD vehicle. During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemiya no Watashi|qq=}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Shinshiro|qq=}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama-juku|qq=}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arato Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Mount Minakami|lt=Mount Minakami|lt=Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami-yama|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizu-hime-no-Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) - revision.text:
{{Infobox mountain | Name = Saijo-san (妻女山) | image = [[Image:Saijyosan 1.jpg|300px|Saijo-san]] | image Caption = View of Saijo-san (Akasaka-yama) from the direction of [[Iwano Station]] | Elevation = 411 m | Coordinates = {{Coord|36|33|40.7|N|138|10|17.2|E|type:mountain_region:JP-20|display=inline,title}} | Location = {{JPN}}
[[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]] | Mountain Range = | Type = | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|36.5613|138.1714}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Saijo-san }} '''Saijo-san''' (妻女山) is a mountain located on the border between {{ill|Matsushiro, Nagano|ja|松代町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushiro, Nagano|qq=}} in [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], and [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma City]], Tsuchiguchi. It is best known as the location where [[:en:Uesugi Kenshin|Uesugi Kenshin]] established his encampment during the [[:en:Battles of Kawanakajima|Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima]]. However, alternative theories suggest the true encampment was on '''Saijo-zan''' (斎場山). == Overview == Saijo-san is situated in a strategic location overlooking {{ill|Kawanakajima|ja|川中島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawanakajima|qq=}} and {{ill|Kaizu Castle|ja|海津城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizu Castle|qq=}}, which was built by {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|qq=}}. It is said that Uesugi Kenshin observed the rising smoke from Kaizu Castle’s many cooking fires and discerned the movements of the Takeda forces. He then secretly descended from the mountain under the cover of night, crossing the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikuma River|qq=}} via the {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemiya no Watashi|qq=}} to outflank his enemy. According to legend, Uesugi’s troops performed [[:en:Sarugaku|Sarugaku]] on an ancient Kofun at the summit, and he lodged at {{ill|Ruriji Temple|ja|瑠璃寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ruriji Temple|qq=}}, a temple along the gentle western slope toward {{ill|Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|土口将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|qq=}}. The temple was later burned down by the Takeda forces after the battle. Currently, the location labeled as "Saijo-san" on maps—where an observation deck and battle explanation panels are installed—is actually a ridge summit of Saijo-zan (historically called "Akasaka" or "Akasaka-yama"). This site was where [[:en:Amakasu Kagemochi|Amakasu Kagemochi]], a rearguard commander in Uesugi’s army, set up his encampment. The site also contains a memorial hall and a monument honoring 960 war dead from the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]] onward. This was built because, during the late Edo period, [[:en:Matsushiro Domain|Matsushiro Domain]] warriors used the area for firearms training, leading to the Matsushiro lords establishing a shrine. Since the Meiji era, this site became a location for large-scale spring and autumn festivals honoring those who contributed to the establishment of the new government. As a result, "going to Saijo-san" became synonymous with visiting this location. However, the {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|qq=}} worship hall was demolished in December 2023. == Naming Controversy == The modern name "Saijo-san" (妻女山, pronounced "Saijozan") became widespread from Edo-period dramas, [[:en:Jōruri (music)|Jōruri]], and [[:en:Kōdan|Kodan]] narratives, which romanticized the story that Uesugi’s warriors gazed northward across Kawanakajima, longing for their distant homeland and mourning their wives and families left behind. Historical records such as the ''[[:en:Kōyō Gunkan|Kōyō Gunkan]]'' refer to the encampment site as "Saijo-yama" (西條山). In the mid-Edo period, the [[:en:Matsushiro Domain|Matsushiro Domain]] (ruled by the [[:en:Sanada clan|Sanada clan]]) criticized this as an error, stating in the ''Shinbu Naiden'' (真武内伝) that "Saijo-yama is not Saijo-san; they are different mountains." Nevertheless, Edo-period maps continued to use the name "Saijo-yama" or "Saijo-san." In reality, the true "Saijo-yama" lies about 10 km to the south. It was used as a signal tower where the Takeda forces sent emergency fire signals to Kai Province when the Uesugi army invaded. It remains unclear why the Matsushiro Domain equated Saijo-yama with Saijo-san. However, some local theories, even today, suggest that Uesugi's true encampment was at "Saijo-zan" (斎場山), a site that contains numerous ancient tombs and was historically used for rituals honoring the dead. Saijo-zan is part of the Togami Mountain Range (also called Kurahone Mountain Range or Omine Mountain Range). It features two ancient fortresses: the upper Kurahone Castle (鞍骨城) and the lower Amagi Castle (天城城, also known as Irayama Castle). The ridge extends east-west, with its highest point being identified as Saijo-zan. However, some maps list the area as "Akasaka" (赤坂) and label "Saijo-san" instead, effectively erasing the original name of the main peak. Some theories support retaining the name "Saijo-san." According to local legend, the Kofun at the peak of Saijo-zan is the grave of {{ill|Aizu-hime|ja|会津比売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime|qq=}}, enshrined at the nearby {{ill|Aizu-hime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime Shrine|qq=}}. Locals also refer to the peak as "Tenjo" (天上) or "Otenjo" (御天上). Aizu-hime is believed to be the granddaughter of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神|1=Takeminakata|2=ja|3=タケミナカタ|lt=Takeminakata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata|qq=}} and the wife of {{ill|Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|qq=}}, the legendary first {{ill|Governor of Shinano|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Shinano|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, who is associated with the {{ill|Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|qq=}}. This theory argues that the name "Saijo-san" is justified in this context. == Alternative Historical Interpretation == While it is commonly believed that Uesugi Kenshin encamped on Saijo-san, some scholars argue that there is no historical evidence for this. They propose that it is a case of mistaken history, confused with a later military action. In March [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 10 (1582), after [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]'s conquest of the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]], [[:en:Mori Nagayoshi|Mori Nagayoshi]] briefly occupied northern Shinano but withdrew following the [[:en:Honnō-ji Incident|Honnō-ji Incident]]. In the ensuing {{ill|Tenshō Jingo War|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Jingo War|qq=}}, as [[:en:Hōjō Ujinao|Hōjō Ujimasa]] advanced northward, [[:en:Uesugi Kagekatsu|Uesugi Kagekatsu]] moved from {{ill|Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|ja|長沼城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|qq=}} to Kaizu Castle, establishing defensive positions at Saijo-san, Kurahone Castle, and Akazawa-yama. This forced the Hōjō forces to retreat and negotiate peace. Some historians argue that later records mistakenly conflated this event with the Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima. == Access and Wildlife == A paved road leads up to Akasaka Observation Deck, making it accessible by regular vehicles. Along the way, visitors may encounter [[:en:Japanese serow|Japanese serow]], and tree trunks bear marks from wild {{ill|boar|ja|猪|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boar|qq=}} rubbing against them. While an unpaved road continues toward Saijo-san’s summit, it is difficult to traverse without a 4WD vehicle. During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemiya no Watashi|qq=}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Shinshiro|qq=}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama-juku|qq=}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arato Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Mount Minakami|lt=Mount Minakami|lt=Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami-yama|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizu-hime-no-Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) - [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]] | Mountain Range = | Type = | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|36.5613|138.1714}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Saijo-san }} '''Saijo-san''' (妻女山) is a mountain located on the border between {{ill|Matsushiro, Nagano|ja|松代町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushiro, Nagano|qq=}} in [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], and [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma City]], Tsuchiguchi. According to local legend, the Kofun at the peak of Saijo-zan is the grave of {{ill|Aizu-hime|ja|会津比売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime|qq=}}, enshrined at the nearby {{ill|Aizu-hime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime Shrine|qq=}}. Locals also refer to the peak as "Tenjo" (天上) or "Otenjo" (御天上). Aizu-hime is believed to be the granddaughter of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神|1=Takeminakata|2=ja|3=タケミナカタ|lt=Takeminakata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata|qq=}} and the wife of {{ill|Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|qq=}}, the legendary first {{ill|Governor of Shinano|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Shinano|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, who is associated with the {{ill|Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|qq=}}. This theory argues that the name "Saijo-san" is justified in this context. == Alternative Historical Interpretation == While it is commonly believed that Uesugi Kenshin encamped on Saijo-san, some scholars argue that there is no historical evidence for this. They propose that it is a case of mistaken history, confused with a later military action. In March [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 10 (1582), after [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]'s conquest of the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]], [[:en:Mori Nagayoshi|Mori Nagayoshi]] briefly occupied northern Shinano but withdrew following the [[:en:Honnō-ji Incident|Honnō-ji Incident]]. In the ensuing {{ill|Tenshō Jingo War|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Jingo War|qq=}}, as [[:en:Hōjō Ujinao|Hōjō Ujimasa]] advanced northward, [[:en:Uesugi Kagekatsu|Uesugi Kagekatsu]] moved from {{ill|Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|ja|長沼城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|qq=}} to Kaizu Castle, establishing defensive positions at Saijo-san, Kurahone Castle, and Akazawa-yama. This forced the Hōjō forces to retreat and negotiate peace. Some historians argue that later records mistakenly conflated this event with the Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima. == Access and Wildlife == A paved road leads up to Akasaka Observation Deck, making it accessible by regular vehicles. Along the way, visitors may encounter [[:en:Japanese serow|Japanese serow]], and tree trunks bear marks from wild {{ill|boar|ja|猪|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boar|qq=}} rubbing against them. While an unpaved road continues toward Saijo-san’s summit, it is difficult to traverse without a 4WD vehicle. During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemiya no Watashi|qq=}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Shinshiro|qq=}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama-juku|qq=}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arato Castle|qq=}} * {{ill|Mount Minakami|lt=Mount Minakami|lt=Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami-yama|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizu-hime-no-Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) -
