[[ja:妻女山]] [[ja:妻女山]] [[ja:妻女山]] {{Infobox mountain | Name = Saijo-san (妻女山) | image = [[Image:Saijyosan 1.jpg|300px|Saijo-san]] | image Caption = View of Saijo-san (Akasaka-yama) from the direction of [[Iwano Station]] | Elevation = 411 m | Coordinates = {{Coord|36|33|40.7|N|138|10|17.2|E|type:mountain_region:JP-20|display=inline,title}} | Location = {{JPN}}
{{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, {{ill|Nagano (city)|en|Nagano (city)|WD=Q128849|lt=Nagano City|1=Nagano (city)}} | Mountain Range = | Type = | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|36.5613|138.1714}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Saijo-san }} '''Saijo-san''' (妻女山) is a mountain located on the border between {{ill|Matsushiro, Nagano|ja|松代町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushiro, Nagano|1=松代町|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11529236}} in {{ill|Nagano (city)|en|Nagano (city)|WD=Q128849|lt=Nagano City|1=Nagano (city)}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, and {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|en|Chikuma, Nagano|WD=Q179757|lt=Chikuma City|1=Chikuma, Nagano}}, Tsuchiguchi. It is best known as the location where {{ill|Uesugi Kenshin|en|Uesugi Kenshin|WD=Q311080|lt=Uesugi Kenshin|1=Uesugi Kenshin}} established his encampment during the {{ill|Battles of Kawanakajima|en|Battles of Kawanakajima|WD=Q1143044|lt=Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima|1=Battles of Kawanakajima}}. However, alternative theories suggest the true encampment was on '''Saijo-zan''' (斎場山). == Overview == Saijo-san is situated in a strategic location overlooking {{ill|Kawanakajima|ja|川中島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawanakajima|1=Kawanakajima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11054941}} and {{ill|Kaizu Castle|ja|海津城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizu Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松代城|1=Matsushiro Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11104236}}, which was built by [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is said that Uesugi Kenshin observed the rising smoke from Kaizu Castle’s many cooking fires and discerned the movements of the Takeda forces. He then secretly descended from the mountain under the cover of night, crossing the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikuma River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 信濃川|1=Shinano River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q734455}} via the {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemiya no Watashi|1=Amenomiya no Watashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659678}} to outflank his enemy. It features two ancient fortresses: the upper Kurahone Castle (鞍骨城) and the lower Amagi Castle (天城城, also known as Irayama Castle). The ridge extends east-west, with its highest point being identified as Saijo-zan. However, some maps list the area as "Akasaka" (赤坂) and label "Saijo-san" instead, effectively erasing the original name of the main peak. Some theories support retaining the name "Saijo-san." According to local legend, the Kofun at the peak of Saijo-zan is the grave of {{ill|Aizu-hime|ja|会津比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, enshrined at the nearby {{ill|Aizu-hime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Locals also refer to the peak as "Tenjo" (天上) or "Otenjo" (御天上). Aizu-hime is believed to be the granddaughter of {{ill|Takeminakata|en|Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata|1=Takeminakata}} and the wife of {{ill|Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|1=Take Momotake no Mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q87863507}}, the legendary first {{ill|Governor of Shinano|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Shinano|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, who is associated with the {{ill|Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群|1=Hanishina Kofun group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6912108}}. This theory argues that the name "Saijo-san" is justified in this context. == Alternative Historical Interpretation == While it is commonly believed that Uesugi Kenshin encamped on Saijo-san, some scholars argue that there is no historical evidence for this. They propose that it is a case of mistaken history, confused with a later military action. In March {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 10 (1582), after {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}}'s conquest of the {{ill|Takeda clan|en|Takeda clan|WD=Q1210114|lt=Takeda clan|1=Takeda clan}}, {{ill|Mori Nagayoshi|en|Mori Nagayoshi|WD=Q1071391|lt=Mori Nagayoshi|1=Mori Nagayoshi}} briefly occupied northern Shinano but withdrew following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|en|Honnō-ji Incident|WD=Q169598|lt=Honnō-ji Incident|1=Honnō-ji Incident}}. In the ensuing {{ill|Tenshō Jingo War|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Jingo War|1=Tenshō-Jingo war|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939819}}, as {{ill|Hōjō Ujinao|en|Hōjō Ujinao|WD=Q1156668|lt=Hōjō Ujimasa|1=Hōjō Ujinao}} advanced northward, {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|en|Uesugi Kagekatsu|WD=Q1376605|lt=Uesugi Kagekatsu|1=Uesugi Kagekatsu}} moved from {{ill|Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|ja|長沼城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|1=Q11653201|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653201}} to Kaizu Castle, establishing defensive positions at Saijo-san, Kurahone Castle, and Akazawa-yama. This forced the Hōjō forces to retreat and negotiate peace. Some historians argue that later records mistakenly conflated this event with the Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima. == Access and Wildlife == A paved road leads up to Akasaka Observation Deck, making it accessible by regular vehicles. Along the way, visitors may encounter {{ill|Japanese serow|en|Japanese serow|WD=Q754034|lt=Japanese serow|1=Japanese serow}}, and tree trunks bear marks from wild {{ill|boar|ja|猪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boar|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イノシシ|1=Wild boar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q58697}} rubbing against them. While an unpaved road continues toward Saijo-san’s summit, it is difficult to traverse without a 4WD vehicle. During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemiya no Watashi|1=Amenomiya no Watashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659678}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Castle|1=Yashiro Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11465653}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Castle|1=Shiozaki Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17228702}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Shinshiro|1=Q11428704|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11428704}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama-juku|1=Inariyama-juku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596867}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arato Castle|1=Arato Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618817}} * {{ill|Mount Minakami|lt=Mount Minakami|lt=Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami-yama|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizu-hime-no-Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) - [[ja:妻女山]] [[ja:妻女山]] [[ja:妻女山]] {{Infobox mountain | Name = Saijo-san (妻女山) | image = [[Image:Saijyosan 1.jpg|300px|Saijo-san]] | image Caption = View of Saijo-san (Akasaka-yama) from the direction of [[Iwano Station]] | Elevation = 411 m | Coordinates = {{Coord|36|33|40.7|N|138|10|17.2|E|type:mountain_region:JP-20|display=inline,title}} | Location = {{JPN}}
{{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, {{ill|Nagano (city)|en|Nagano (city)|WD=Q128849|lt=Nagano City|1=Nagano (city)}} | Mountain Range = | Type = | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|36.5613|138.1714}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Saijo-san }} '''Saijo-san''' (妻女山) is a mountain located on the border between {{ill|Matsushiro, Nagano|ja|松代町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushiro, Nagano|1=松代町|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11529236}} in {{ill|Nagano (city)|en|Nagano (city)|WD=Q128849|lt=Nagano City|1=Nagano (city)}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, and {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|en|Chikuma, Nagano|WD=Q179757|lt=Chikuma City|1=Chikuma, Nagano}}, Tsuchiguchi. It is best known as the location where {{ill|Uesugi Kenshin|en|Uesugi Kenshin|WD=Q311080|lt=Uesugi Kenshin|1=Uesugi Kenshin}} established his encampment during the {{ill|Battles of Kawanakajima|en|Battles of Kawanakajima|WD=Q1143044|lt=Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima|1=Battles of Kawanakajima}}. However, alternative theories suggest the true encampment was on '''Saijo-zan''' (斎場山). == Overview == Saijo-san is situated in a strategic location overlooking {{ill|Kawanakajima|ja|川中島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawanakajima|1=Kawanakajima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11054941}} and {{ill|Kaizu Castle|ja|海津城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizu Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松代城|1=Matsushiro Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11104236}}, which was built by [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is said that Uesugi Kenshin observed the rising smoke from Kaizu Castle’s many cooking fires and discerned the movements of the Takeda forces. He then secretly descended from the mountain under the cover of night, crossing the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikuma River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 信濃川|1=Shinano River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q734455}} via the {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemiya no Watashi|1=Amenomiya no Watashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659678}} to outflank his enemy. According to legend, Uesugi’s troops performed {{ill|Sarugaku|en|Sarugaku|WD=Q1204318|lt=Sarugaku|1=Sarugaku}} on an ancient Kofun at the summit, and he lodged at {{ill|Ruriji Temple|ja|瑠璃寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ruriji Temple|1=Q11573991|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573991}}, a temple along the gentle western slope toward {{ill|Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|土口将軍塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群|1=Hanishina Kofun group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6912108}}. The temple was later burned down by the Takeda forces after the battle. Currently, the location labeled as "Saijo-san" on maps—where an observation deck and battle explanation panels are installed—is actually a ridge summit of Saijo-zan (historically called "Akasaka" or "Akasaka-yama"). This site was where {{ill|Amakasu Kagemochi|en|Amakasu Kagemochi|WD=Q866051|lt=Amakasu Kagemochi|1=Amakasu Kagemochi}}, a rearguard commander in Uesugi’s army, set up his encampment. The site also contains a memorial hall and a monument honoring 960 war dead from the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War|1=Boshin War}} onward. This was built because, during the late Edo period, {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|en|Matsushiro Domain|WD=Q903575|lt=Matsushiro Domain|1=Matsushiro Domain}} warriors used the area for firearms training, leading to the Matsushiro lords establishing a shrine. Since the Meiji era, this site became a location for large-scale spring and autumn festivals honoring those who contributed to the establishment of the new government. As a result, "going to Saijo-san" became synonymous with visiting this location. However, the {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810}} worship hall was demolished in December 2023. == Naming Controversy == The modern name "Saijo-san" (妻女山, pronounced "Saijozan") became widespread from Edo-period dramas, {{ill|Jōruri (music)|en|Jōruri (music)|WD=Q1361891|lt=Jōruri|1=Jōruri (music)}}, and {{ill|Kōdan|en|Kōdan|WD=Q3201265|lt=Kodan|1=Kōdan}} narratives, which romanticized the story that Uesugi’s warriors gazed northward across Kawanakajima, longing for their distant homeland and mourning their wives and families left behind. Historical records such as the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|en|Kōyō Gunkan|WD=Q586111|lt=Kōyō Gunkan|1=Kōyō Gunkan}}'' refer to the encampment site as "Saijo-yama" (西條山). In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|en|Matsushiro Domain|WD=Q903575|lt=Matsushiro Domain|1=Matsushiro Domain}} (ruled by the {{ill|Sanada clan|en|Sanada clan|WD=Q1002844|lt=Sanada clan|1=Sanada clan}}) criticized this as an error, stating in the ''Shinbu Naiden'' (真武内伝) that "Saijo-yama is not Saijo-san; they are different mountains." Nevertheless, Edo-period maps continued to use the name "Saijo-yama" or "Saijo-san." In reality, the true "Saijo-yama" lies about 10 km to the south. It was used as a signal tower where the Takeda forces sent emergency fire signals to Kai Province when the Uesugi army invaded. It remains unclear why the Matsushiro Domain equated Saijo-yama with Saijo-san. However, some local theories, even today, suggest that Uesugi's true encampment was at "Saijo-zan" (斎場山), a site that contains numerous ancient tombs and was historically used for rituals honoring the dead. Saijo-zan is part of the Togami Mountain Range (also called Kurahone Mountain Range or Omine Mountain Range). It features two ancient fortresses: the upper Kurahone Castle (鞍骨城) and the lower Amagi Castle (天城城, also known as Irayama Castle). The ridge extends east-west, with its highest point being identified as Saijo-zan. However, some maps list the area as "Akasaka" (赤坂) and label "Saijo-san" instead, effectively erasing the original name of the main peak. Some theories support retaining the name "Saijo-san." According to local legend, the Kofun at the peak of Saijo-zan is the grave of {{ill|Aizu-hime|ja|会津比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, enshrined at the nearby {{ill|Aizu-hime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Locals also refer to the peak as "Tenjo" (天上) or "Otenjo" (御天上). Aizu-hime is believed to be the granddaughter of {{ill|Takeminakata|en|Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata|1=Takeminakata}} and the wife of {{ill|Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|1=Take Momotake no Mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q87863507}}, the legendary first {{ill|Governor of Shinano|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Shinano|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, who is associated with the {{ill|Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群|1=Hanishina Kofun group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6912108}}. This theory argues that the name "Saijo-san" is justified in this context. == Alternative Historical Interpretation == While it is commonly believed that Uesugi Kenshin encamped on Saijo-san, some scholars argue that there is no historical evidence for this. They propose that it is a case of mistaken history, confused with a later military action. In March {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 10 (1582), after {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}}'s conquest of the {{ill|Takeda clan|en|Takeda clan|WD=Q1210114|lt=Takeda clan|1=Takeda clan}}, {{ill|Mori Nagayoshi|en|Mori Nagayoshi|WD=Q1071391|lt=Mori Nagayoshi|1=Mori Nagayoshi}} briefly occupied northern Shinano but withdrew following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|en|Honnō-ji Incident|WD=Q169598|lt=Honnō-ji Incident|1=Honnō-ji Incident}}. In the ensuing {{ill|Tenshō Jingo War|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Jingo War|1=Tenshō-Jingo war|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939819}}, as {{ill|Hōjō Ujinao|en|Hōjō Ujinao|WD=Q1156668|lt=Hōjō Ujimasa|1=Hōjō Ujinao}} advanced northward, {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|en|Uesugi Kagekatsu|WD=Q1376605|lt=Uesugi Kagekatsu|1=Uesugi Kagekatsu}} moved from {{ill|Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|ja|長沼城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|1=Q11653201|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653201}} to Kaizu Castle, establishing defensive positions at Saijo-san, Kurahone Castle, and Akazawa-yama. This forced the Hōjō forces to retreat and negotiate peace. Some historians argue that later records mistakenly conflated this event with the Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima. == Access and Wildlife == A paved road leads up to Akasaka Observation Deck, making it accessible by regular vehicles. Along the way, visitors may encounter {{ill|Japanese serow|en|Japanese serow|WD=Q754034|lt=Japanese serow|1=Japanese serow}}, and tree trunks bear marks from wild {{ill|boar|ja|猪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boar|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イノシシ|1=Wild boar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q58697}} rubbing against them. While an unpaved road continues toward Saijo-san’s summit, it is difficult to traverse without a 4WD vehicle. During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemiya no Watashi|1=Amenomiya no Watashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659678}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Castle|1=Yashiro Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11465653}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Castle|1=Shiozaki Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17228702}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Shinshiro|1=Q11428704|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11428704}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama-juku|1=Inariyama-juku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596867}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arato Castle|1=Arato Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618817}} * {{ill|Mount Minakami|lt=Mount Minakami|lt=Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami-yama|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizu-hime-no-Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) - revision.text:
{{Infobox mountain | Name = Saijo-san (妻女山) | image = [[Image:Saijyosan 1.jpg|300px|Saijo-san]] | image Caption = View of Saijo-san (Akasaka-yama) from the direction of [[Iwano Station]] | Elevation = 411 m | Coordinates = {{Coord|36|33|40.7|N|138|10|17.2|E|type:mountain_region:JP-20|display=inline,title}} | Location = {{JPN}}
{{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, {{ill|Nagano (city)|en|Nagano (city)|WD=Q128849|lt=Nagano City|1=Nagano (city)}} | Mountain Range = | Type = | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|36.5613|138.1714}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Saijo-san }} '''Saijo-san''' (妻女山) is a mountain located on the border between {{ill|Matsushiro, Nagano|ja|松代町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushiro, Nagano|1=松代町|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11529236}} in {{ill|Nagano (city)|en|Nagano (city)|WD=Q128849|lt=Nagano City|1=Nagano (city)}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, and {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|en|Chikuma, Nagano|WD=Q179757|lt=Chikuma City|1=Chikuma, Nagano}}, Tsuchiguchi. It is best known as the location where {{ill|Uesugi Kenshin|en|Uesugi Kenshin|WD=Q311080|lt=Uesugi Kenshin|1=Uesugi Kenshin}} established his encampment during the {{ill|Battles of Kawanakajima|en|Battles of Kawanakajima|WD=Q1143044|lt=Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima|1=Battles of Kawanakajima}}. However, alternative theories suggest the true encampment was on '''Saijo-zan''' (斎場山). == Overview == Saijo-san is situated in a strategic location overlooking {{ill|Kawanakajima|ja|川中島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawanakajima|1=Kawanakajima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11054941}} and {{ill|Kaizu Castle|ja|海津城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizu Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松代城|1=Matsushiro Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11104236}}, which was built by [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is said that Uesugi Kenshin observed the rising smoke from Kaizu Castle’s many cooking fires and discerned the movements of the Takeda forces. He then secretly descended from the mountain under the cover of night, crossing the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikuma River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 信濃川|1=Shinano River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q734455}} via the {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemiya no Watashi|1=Amenomiya no Watashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659678}} to outflank his enemy. According to legend, Uesugi’s troops performed {{ill|Sarugaku|en|Sarugaku|WD=Q1204318|lt=Sarugaku|1=Sarugaku}} on an ancient Kofun at the summit, and he lodged at {{ill|Ruriji Temple|ja|瑠璃寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ruriji Temple|1=Q11573991|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573991}}, a temple along the gentle western slope toward {{ill|Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|土口将軍塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchiguchi Shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群|1=Hanishina Kofun group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6912108}}. The temple was later burned down by the Takeda forces after the battle. Currently, the location labeled as "Saijo-san" on maps—where an observation deck and battle explanation panels are installed—is actually a ridge summit of Saijo-zan (historically called "Akasaka" or "Akasaka-yama"). This site was where {{ill|Amakasu Kagemochi|en|Amakasu Kagemochi|WD=Q866051|lt=Amakasu Kagemochi|1=Amakasu Kagemochi}}, a rearguard commander in Uesugi’s army, set up his encampment. The site also contains a memorial hall and a monument honoring 960 war dead from the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War|1=Boshin War}} onward. This was built because, during the late Edo period, {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|en|Matsushiro Domain|WD=Q903575|lt=Matsushiro Domain|1=Matsushiro Domain}} warriors used the area for firearms training, leading to the Matsushiro lords establishing a shrine. Since the Meiji era, this site became a location for large-scale spring and autumn festivals honoring those who contributed to the establishment of the new government. As a result, "going to Saijo-san" became synonymous with visiting this location. However, the {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810}} worship hall was demolished in December 2023. == Naming Controversy == The modern name "Saijo-san" (妻女山, pronounced "Saijozan") became widespread from Edo-period dramas, {{ill|Jōruri (music)|en|Jōruri (music)|WD=Q1361891|lt=Jōruri|1=Jōruri (music)}}, and {{ill|Kōdan|en|Kōdan|WD=Q3201265|lt=Kodan|1=Kōdan}} narratives, which romanticized the story that Uesugi’s warriors gazed northward across Kawanakajima, longing for their distant homeland and mourning their wives and families left behind. Historical records such as the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|en|Kōyō Gunkan|WD=Q586111|lt=Kōyō Gunkan|1=Kōyō Gunkan}}'' refer to the encampment site as "Saijo-yama" (西條山). In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|en|Matsushiro Domain|WD=Q903575|lt=Matsushiro Domain|1=Matsushiro Domain}} (ruled by the {{ill|Sanada clan|en|Sanada clan|WD=Q1002844|lt=Sanada clan|1=Sanada clan}}) criticized this as an error, stating in the ''Shinbu Naiden'' (真武内伝) that "Saijo-yama is not Saijo-san; they are different mountains." Nevertheless, Edo-period maps continued to use the name "Saijo-yama" or "Saijo-san." In reality, the true "Saijo-yama" lies about 10 km to the south. It was used as a signal tower where the Takeda forces sent emergency fire signals to Kai Province when the Uesugi army invaded. It remains unclear why the Matsushiro Domain equated Saijo-yama with Saijo-san. However, some local theories, even today, suggest that Uesugi's true encampment was at "Saijo-zan" (斎場山), a site that contains numerous ancient tombs and was historically used for rituals honoring the dead. Saijo-zan is part of the Togami Mountain Range (also called Kurahone Mountain Range or Omine Mountain Range). It features two ancient fortresses: the upper Kurahone Castle (鞍骨城) and the lower Amagi Castle (天城城, also known as Irayama Castle). The ridge extends east-west, with its highest point being identified as Saijo-zan. However, some maps list the area as "Akasaka" (赤坂) and label "Saijo-san" instead, effectively erasing the original name of the main peak. Some theories support retaining the name "Saijo-san." According to local legend, the Kofun at the peak of Saijo-zan is the grave of {{ill|Aizu-hime|ja|会津比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, enshrined at the nearby {{ill|Aizu-hime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizu-hime Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Locals also refer to the peak as "Tenjo" (天上) or "Otenjo" (御天上). Aizu-hime is believed to be the granddaughter of {{ill|Takeminakata|en|Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata|1=Takeminakata}} and the wife of {{ill|Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeiho-tatenu no Mikoto|1=Take Momotake no Mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q87863507}}, the legendary first {{ill|Governor of Shinano|ja|科野国造|1=Shinano no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor of Shinano|13=User:Immanuelle/Shina no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, who is associated with the {{ill|Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群|1=Hanishina Kofun group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6912108}}. This theory argues that the name "Saijo-san" is justified in this context. == Alternative Historical Interpretation == While it is commonly believed that Uesugi Kenshin encamped on Saijo-san, some scholars argue that there is no historical evidence for this. They propose that it is a case of mistaken history, confused with a later military action. In March {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 10 (1582), after {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}}'s conquest of the {{ill|Takeda clan|en|Takeda clan|WD=Q1210114|lt=Takeda clan|1=Takeda clan}}, {{ill|Mori Nagayoshi|en|Mori Nagayoshi|WD=Q1071391|lt=Mori Nagayoshi|1=Mori Nagayoshi}} briefly occupied northern Shinano but withdrew following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|en|Honnō-ji Incident|WD=Q169598|lt=Honnō-ji Incident|1=Honnō-ji Incident}}. In the ensuing {{ill|Tenshō Jingo War|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Jingo War|1=Tenshō-Jingo war|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939819}}, as {{ill|Hōjō Ujinao|en|Hōjō Ujinao|WD=Q1156668|lt=Hōjō Ujimasa|1=Hōjō Ujinao}} advanced northward, {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|en|Uesugi Kagekatsu|WD=Q1376605|lt=Uesugi Kagekatsu|1=Uesugi Kagekatsu}} moved from {{ill|Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|ja|長沼城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naganuma Castle (Shinano)|1=Q11653201|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653201}} to Kaizu Castle, establishing defensive positions at Saijo-san, Kurahone Castle, and Akazawa-yama. This forced the Hōjō forces to retreat and negotiate peace. Some historians argue that later records mistakenly conflated this event with the Fourth Battle of Kawanakajima. == Access and Wildlife == A paved road leads up to Akasaka Observation Deck, making it accessible by regular vehicles. Along the way, visitors may encounter {{ill|Japanese serow|en|Japanese serow|WD=Q754034|lt=Japanese serow|1=Japanese serow}}, and tree trunks bear marks from wild {{ill|boar|ja|猪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boar|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イノシシ|1=Wild boar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q58697}} rubbing against them. While an unpaved road continues toward Saijo-san’s summit, it is difficult to traverse without a 4WD vehicle. During summer, bees are prevalent, and tree growth obstructs the view. == Related Sites == * {{ill|Amemiya no Watashi|ja|雨宮の渡し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amemiya no Watashi|1=Amenomiya no Watashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659678}} * {{ill|Yashiro Castle|ja|屋代城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Castle|1=Yashiro Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11465653}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Castle|ja|塩崎城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Castle|1=Shiozaki Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17228702}} * {{ill|Shiozaki Shinshiro|ja|塩崎新城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Shinshiro|1=Q11428704|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11428704}} * {{ill|Inariyama-juku|ja|稲荷山宿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inariyama-juku|1=Inariyama-juku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11596867}} * {{ill|Arato Castle|ja|荒砥城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arato Castle|1=Arato Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618817}} * {{ill|Mount Minakami|lt=Mount Minakami|lt=Minakami-yama|ja|皆神山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami-yama|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.furin-kazan.jp/nagano/shiseki/entry/000608.php 長野市「信州・風林火山」特設サイト] (Archived) * [[Aizu-hime-no-Kami]] | burial_place = {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}} A legend claims that her tomb lies atop {{ill|Tsumaonna Mountain|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumaonna Mountain|qq=}}, and the mountain's name is said to originate from her story. ... 10 KB (1,096 words) -
